# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step in this section is shown explicitly. KTU examiners award partial credit for the **derivation logic**, not just the final answer. Students who skip intermediate steps lose marks.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Laser Threshold Condition

We start with the **Schawlow–Townes threshold criterion**: lasing begins when the round-trip gain in the cavity equals the round-trip losses.

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip gain.**
A signal of intensity $I$ traveling once through a fiber of length $L$ experiences a net gain:

$$
G_1 = e^{gL}
$$

where $g$ is the small-signal gain coefficient (in $\text{m}^{-1}$). After one full round trip (forward then back), the intensity becomes:

$$
G_{RT} = e^{2gL}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the round-trip loss.**
The loss in a single pass comes from mirror transmission at the output coupler (transmissivity $T$) and internal cavity loss $\alpha_{int}$:

$$
L_1 = T + \alpha_{int}
$$

After a round trip:

$$
L_{RT} = 1 - (1-T)(1-\alpha_{int}) \approx 2T + 2\alpha_{int} \quad \text{(small loss)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state threshold condition.**

$$
\text{Gain} = \text{Loss}
$$

$$
e^{2gL} = \dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}
$$

Here, $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the reflectivities of the two cavity mirrors, and the product $R_1 R_2$ is the fraction of light surviving the round trip.

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the threshold gain $g_{th}$.**

$$
2g_{th} L = -\ln(R_1 R_2) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$

$$
g_{th} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the threshold population inversion.**
Using the gain expression $g = \Gamma(\sigma_{em} N_2 - \sigma_{abs} N_1)$ where $\Gamma$ is the overlap (confinement) factor between the mode and the doped core:

$$
g_{th} = \Gamma(\sigma_{em} N_2 - \sigma_{abs} N_1)
$$

Solving for $N_2$ at threshold (with $N_1 \approx N_{tot} - N_2$ at room temperature and for simplicity assuming $N_1$ small):

$$
N_2^{th} = \dfrac{g_{th}}{\Gamma \sigma_{em}} = \dfrac{\ln(1/R_1 R_2)}{2L\ \Gamma\ \sigma_{em}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This result explains a key engineering fact: **longer cavities require lower inversion density per unit length** to reach threshold. This is one of the reasons fiber lasers can reach threshold at very low pump powers — the effective interaction length $L$ can be many meters.

## 3.2 Derivation of Output Power vs. Pump Power (Slope Equation)

Above threshold, the output power grows linearly with pump power. We derive this from a power balance.

**Step 1 — Above threshold, the inversion is clamped at $N_2 = N_2^{th}$.**
The pump power in excess of threshold is converted into laser photons.

**Step 2 — Define the quantum efficiency.**
Each pump photon of energy $h\nu_P$ produces one laser photon of energy $h\nu_L$ (Stokes efficiency $\eta_{St} = h\nu_L / h\nu_P = \lambda_P / \lambda_L$).

**Step 3 — Define the slope efficiency.**
The fraction of additional pump photons that emerge as output photons is:

$$
\eta_s = \eta_{St} \cdot \eta_{QE} \cdot \eta_{out}
$$

where $\eta_{QE}$ is the pump quantum efficiency (fraction of absorbed pump photons that contribute to inversion) and $\eta_{out}$ is the output coupling efficiency (fraction of intracavity photons that exit as useful laser light).

**Step 4 — Write the output power.**
For a pump power $P_{in}$ above threshold $P_{th}$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{out} = \eta_s\,(P_{in} - P_{th})\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
This straight-line equation is the **laser characteristic curve**. The $x$-intercept is $P_{th}$ and the slope is $\eta_s$. KTU examiners frequently plot this curve and ask the student to extract $P_{th}$ and $\eta_s$ from data.

## 3.3 Derivation of Pump Threshold Power

**Step 1 — Use the metastable-level rate equation in steady state.**

$$
\dfrac{dN_2}{dt} = W_P N_1 - \dfrac{N_2}{\tau} - W_S(N_2 - N_1) = 0
$$

where $W_P$ is the pump rate per ion, $\tau$ is the metastable lifetime, and $W_S$ is the stimulated emission rate from the laser field.

**Step 2 — At threshold, the laser field is zero, so $W_S \approx 0$.**

$$
W_P N_1 = \dfrac{N_2}{\tau}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $N_1 = N_{tot} - N_2$ and solve for the threshold pump rate.**

$$
W_P^{th} = \dfrac{N_2^{th}}{\tau(N_{tot} - N_2^{th})}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert pump rate to pump power.**
The pump rate per ion is $W_P = \sigma_{P,a} I_P / h\nu_P$. For a fiber of core area $A$ and length $L$ with $N_1 \approx N_{tot}$ (low inversion):

$$
P_{th} = \dfrac{h\nu_P \cdot A \cdot N_2^{th}}{\sigma_{P,a}\ \tau}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression shows why **long-lived metastable levels** (e.g., $10\ \text{ms}$ for $\text{Er}^{3+}$) reduce the threshold pump power dramatically — $\tau$ appears in the denominator.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> [!TIP]
> A typical KTU Part B question is a numerical on the laser characteristic curve. We solve one fully below.

**Problem.** A 5-m-long erbium-doped fiber laser is pumped at $980\ \text{nm}$ and emits at $1550\ \text{nm}$. The slope efficiency is $65\%$ and the threshold pump power is $12\ \text{mW}$. The maximum available pump power is $200\ \text{mW}$. Find:
1. The output power at the maximum pump.
2. The Stokes efficiency.
3. The pump power at which the output is $50\ \text{mW}$.

**Solution.**

**(1)** From the slope equation:
$$
P_{out} = \eta_s (P_{in} - P_{th}) = 0.65 \times (200 - 12) = 0.65 \times 188
$$
$$
P_{out} = 122.2\ \text{mW}
$$

**(2)** Stokes efficiency:
$$
\eta_{St} = \dfrac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_L} = \dfrac{980}{1550} = 0.6322 = 63.22\%
$$

**(3)** For $P_{out} = 50\ \text{mW}$:
$$
50 = 0.65 \times (P_{in} - 12) \Rightarrow P_{in} - 12 = \dfrac{50}{0.65} = 76.92\ \text{mW}
$$
$$
P_{in} = 76.92 + 12 = 88.92\ \text{mW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing $P_{th}$ as the minimum *output* power rather than the minimum *pump* power. $P_{th}$ is always defined on the **pump-power axis** of the characteristic curve.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Rare-Earth Dopants Used in Fiber Lasers

| Dopant | Pump Wavelength | Laser Wavelength | Metastable Lifetime | Biomedical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{Nd}^{3+}$ | $808\ \text{nm}$ | $1064\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 0.3\ \text{ms}$ | Tissue cutting, ophthalmology |
| $\text{Yb}^{3+}$ | $915/975\ \text{nm}$ | $1030$–$1100\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 0.8\ \text{ms}$ | High-power surgery, multiphoton imaging |
| $\text{Er}^{3+}$ | $980/1480\ \text{nm}$ | $1530$–$1560\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 10\ \text{ms}$ | Eye-safe, dermatology, telecom |
| $\text{Tm}^{3+}$ | $790\ \text{nm}$ | $1900$–$2050\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 0.5\ \text{ms}$ | Mid-IR surgery, lithotripsy |
| $\text{Ho}^{3+}$ | $1150\ \text{nm}$ | $2100\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 8\ \text{ms}$ | Urology, soft tissue ablation |
| $\text{Pr}^{3+}$ | $1018\ \text{nm}$ | $1300\ \text{nm}$ | $\sim 0.5\ \text{ms}$ | Visible fiber lasers (red/green via upconversion) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Physical drawings of fiber cross-sections and resonators are best represented as functional block-architecture diagrams in Mermaid. The diagrams below cover (A) the overall fiber-laser architecture, (B) the pumping and signal flow, and (C) the FBG-based linear resonator in detail.

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Complete Fiber Laser System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[Pump Laser Diode 980 nm]:::pump
    WDM1[Wavelength Division Multiplexer WDM Coupler]:::coupler
    CORE[Erbium Doped Fiber Core 10 m Active Gain Medium]:::gain
    HRG[High Reflector FBG R1 equals 99 percent]:::mirror
    OCG[Output Coupler FBG R2 equals 95 percent]:::mirror
    OUT[Coherent Laser Output 1550 nm]:::output
    ISO[Optical Isolator]:::iso
    CTRL[Temperature and Current Controller]:::ctrl

    CTRL --> PUMP
    PUMP --> WDM1
    WDM1 --> CORE
    CORE --> HRG
    HRG -. reflected .-> CORE
    CORE --> OCG
    OCG --> OUT
    OCG --> ISO
    ISO --> WDM1
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The pump laser injects $980\ \text{nm}$ light through a WDM coupler into the erbium-doped fiber.
- The signal grows in both directions as it bounces between two FBGs written into the fiber ends.
- A fraction leaks through the output FBG as the useful laser beam.
- The optical isolator blocks any back-reflection from disturbing the pump diode.

## 4.2 Linear Fabry-Pérot Resonator — Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAV[FABRY PEROT CAVITY IN DOPED FIBER]
        A1[Left FBG High Reflector R1 equals 0.99]:::m1
        A2[Active Region Er Doped Fiber Length L]:::m2
        A3[Right FBG Output Coupler R2 equals 0.95]:::m3
        A1 <--> A2
        A2 <--> A3
    end
    INJ[Pump Light 980 nm]:::inj
    OUT2[Output Laser 1550 nm]:::out
    INJ --> A1
    A3 --> OUT2
```

## 4.3 End-Pumped vs. Bidirectional vs. Double-Clad Pumping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EP[END PUMPED CONFIGURATION]
        EP1[Pump Diode]:::pump --> EP2[WDM Coupler]:::coupler --> EP3[Doped Fiber]:::gain --> EP4[Output Coupler]:::mirror --> EP5[Laser Output]:::output
    end

    subgraph BP[BIDIRECTIONAL PUMPED CONFIGURATION]
        BP1[Pump Diode 1]:::pump --> BP2[Left WDM]:::coupler
        BP3[Pump Diode 2]:::pump --> BP4[Right WDM]:::coupler
        BP2 --> BP5[Doped Fiber]:::gain
        BP4 --> BP5
        BP5 --> BP6[Output]:::output
    end

    subgraph DC[DOUBLE CLAD FIBER PUMPING]
        DC1[High Power Multimode Pump]:::pump --> DC2[Coupler into Inner Cladding]:::coupler --> DC3[Double Clad Doped Fiber Core 10 micron and Cladding 100 micron]:::gain --> DC4[Output]:::output
    end
```

## 4.4 Energy Level Diagram — Erbium-Doped Fiber Laser (As Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Ground State I15 divided by 2]:::ground
    E1[Pump Excited State I11 divided by 2 at 980 nm]:::p1
    E2[Metastable State I13 divided by 2 at 1550 nm laser level]:::meta
    ABS[Absorption 980 nm pump photon]:::pabs
    DEC[Fast Non Radiative Decay phonon assisted]:::pdec
    STIM[Stimulated Emission 1550 nm laser photon]:::pstim
    FLUO[Spontaneous Fluorescence 1550 nm]:::pfl

    ABS --> E1
    E1 --> DEC
    DEC --> E2
    E2 --> STIM
    E2 --> FLUO
    G --> ABS
    STIM --> G
    FLUO --> G
```

**Reading the energy-level diagram:**
- The ion is excited from the ground state to the pump level by absorbing a $980\ \text{nm}$ photon.
- It drops non-radiatively to the metastable level, which has a long lifetime.
- From the metastable level, it can drop back to the ground state by emitting a $1550\ \text{nm}$ photon, either spontaneously (fluorescence) or stimulated (laser action).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`
**Define an optical fiber laser. Mention any two of its advantages for biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An optical fiber laser is a solid-state laser in which the gain medium is an optical fiber whose core is doped with rare-earth ions such as $\text{Er}^{3+}$, $\text{Nd}^{3+}$, or $\text{Yb}^{3+}$. The pump light and the laser light both propagate within the same fiber waveguide, and a pair of fiber Bragg gratings (FBGs) forms the optical cavity.

> **Any two advantages (1.5 marks):**
> 1. The laser output is delivered through the same fiber used as the gain medium, enabling minimally invasive endoscopic and catheter-based delivery.
> 2. The output beam is diffraction-limited ($M^2 \approx 1$) because the lasing mode is intrinsically single-mode, giving precise cutting in microsurgery.
> 3. The fiber geometry provides excellent surface-area-to-volume cooling, allowing stable CW operation at high power without active cooling.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**What is a fiber Bragg grating (FBG)? How is it used as a cavity mirror in a fiber laser?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **FBG definition (1.5 marks):** A fiber Bragg grating is a periodic modulation of the refractive index written into the core of an optical fiber, typically by exposing a photosensitive fiber to an interference pattern of ultraviolet light. It reflects a specific wavelength (the Bragg wavelength) given by $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$, where $n_{eff}$ is the effective index and $\Lambda$ is the grating period.

> **Use as mirror (1.5 marks):** Two FBGs with different reflectivities (e.g., $99\%$ and $95\%$) are written at the two ends of the doped fiber. They form a monolithic, alignment-free Fabry–Pérot cavity. The high-reflector FBG traps the signal in the cavity; the lower-reflectivity FBG acts as the output coupler, allowing useful laser power to escape. Because the FBGs are wavelength-selective, they also determine the exact lasing wavelength and provide spectral narrowing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Slope Efficiency vs. Quantum Efficiency:**
> Many students write $\eta_s = \lambda_P / \lambda_L$ and call it the "efficiency" — this is wrong. The full expression is $\eta_s = \eta_{QE} \cdot (\lambda_P / \lambda_L) \cdot \eta_{out}$. Omitting either $\eta_{QE}$ or $\eta_{out}$ will cost you 1 to 2 marks. The Stokes factor $\lambda_P / \lambda_L$ is only the *upper bound* (the quantum-defect limit); the actual slope is always lower.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an erbium-doped fiber laser (EDFL). Describe the role of fiber Bragg gratings as cavity reflectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the laser threshold condition and the output-power versus pump-power relation. A 5-m-long EDFL emits at $1550\ \text{nm}$ when pumped at $980\ \text{nm}$. The slope efficiency is $0.6$ and the threshold pump power is $10\ \text{mW}$. Find (i) the output power at $150\ \text{mW}$ pump, and (ii) the Stokes efficiency limit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction and working (7 marks):**

- **Pump source (1 mark):** A semiconductor laser diode at $980\ \text{nm}$ is used as the pump, because $980\ \text{nm}$ matches the $\text{Er}^{3+}$ absorption peak from $^4I_{15/2}$ to $^4I_{11/2}$.
- **WDM coupler (1 mark):** The pump light is combined with the signal wavelength inside a wavelength-division-multiplexing coupler and injected into the erbium-doped fiber.
- **Gain medium (1 mark):** A few meters of erbium-doped single-mode fiber forms the active medium. Erbium ions absorb pump photons, are excited to $^4I_{11/2}$, decay non-radiatively to the metastable $^4I_{13/2}$ level, and create the population inversion needed for stimulated emission at $1530$–$1560\ \text{nm}$.
- **FBG cavity (2 marks):** Two fiber Bragg gratings — a high reflector (HR FBG, $R_1 \approx 99.9\%$) and an output coupler (OC FBG, $R_2 \approx 90$–$95\%$) — are written directly into the fiber. They provide wavelength-selective feedback, fix the lasing wavelength at $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$, and make the cavity monolithic, alignment-free, and immune to vibration.
- **Working (1 mark):** Spontaneous emission seeds the cavity, the FBGs reflect and reinforce the seed photons, stimulated emission amplifies the signal, and a stable CW output emerges through the output coupler.
- **Block diagram (1 mark):** (See Section 4.1 — draw Pump → WDM → EDF → FBG₁ ↔ FBG₂ → Output.)

**(b) Derivation and calculation (7 marks):**

**Derivation of threshold condition (2.5 marks):**
The round-trip gain must equal the round-trip loss. Starting from
$$
e^{2gL} = \frac{1}{R_1 R_2}
$$
take logarithms:
$$
g_{th} = \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$
Equating $g_{th}$ to $\Gamma \sigma_{em} N_2^{th}$ gives the threshold population inversion density.

**Derivation of output power (2 marks):**
Above threshold, the inversion is clamped, and excess pump power is converted to laser output:
$$
P_{out} = \eta_s\,(P_{in} - P_{th})
$$

**Numerical calculation (2.5 marks):**
$$
P_{out} = 0.6 \times (150 - 10) = 0.6 \times 140 = 84\ \text{mW}
$$
$$
\eta_{Stokes} = \frac{980}{1550} = 0.6322 = 63.22\%
$$
Note that the actual slope efficiency ($0.6 = 60\%$) is slightly less than the Stokes limit ($63.22\%$), which is the expected behavior because additional losses (coupling, non-radiative decay, scattering) reduce the actual efficiency below the quantum-defect limit.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of a double-clad fiber. How does it enable high-power fiber lasers? Discuss any two biomedical applications that benefit from this technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fiber laser has the following characteristic: the threshold pump power is $5\ \text{mW}$ and the slope efficiency is $70\%$. The output coupler reflectivity is $R_2 = 0.95$ and the other mirror has $R_1 = 0.99$. The active fiber length is $8\ \text{m}$ and the emission cross-section at the signal wavelength is $\sigma_{em} = 6 \times 10^{-25}\ \text{m}^2$. If the overlap factor is $\Gamma = 0.8$, calculate (i) the threshold gain coefficient, (ii) the threshold inversion density, and (iii) the output power at $100\ \text{mW}$ pump power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Double-clad fiber and applications (7 marks):**

- **Structure (2 marks):** A double-clad fiber has three concentric layers: (i) a single-mode rare-earth-doped **core** ($\sim 10\ \mu\text{m}$ diameter) that guides the signal, (ii) a larger multimode **inner cladding** ($\sim 100\ \mu\text{m}$) that guides the pump, and (iii) a low-index **outer cladding** (polymer) that confines the pump within the inner cladding.
- **Mechanism of high-power operation (2.5 marks):** The pump light is launched into the large inner cladding from a high-power multimode pump diode. As it propagates, the pump undergoes multiple internal reflections (skew rays) and is gradually absorbed by the rare-earth ions in the core. Because the inner cladding has a large area and numerical aperture, very high pump power (tens to hundreds of watts) can be coupled in, while the laser output remains diffraction-limited because it is generated in the single-mode core. This decoupling of pump brightness from signal brightness is the key to high-power fiber lasers.
- **Biomedical applications (2.5 marks):**
  1. *Surgical cutting and ablation*: High-power Yb-fiber lasers at $1070\ \text{nm}$ are used in dentistry, ENT surgery, and veterinary surgery for precise soft-tissue cutting with minimal collateral damage.
  2. *LITHOTRIPSY*: Thulium-doped fiber lasers at $1940\ \text{nm}$ are highly absorbed by water and are used to fragment urinary and kidney stones.
  3. *Selective retinal therapy*: High-power yellow (578 nm) fiber lasers, obtained by frequency doubling of an Yb-fiber source, are used in ophthalmology to treat retinal vascular diseases.

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Threshold gain coefficient (2 marks):**
$$
g_{th} = \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) = \frac{1}{2 \times 8}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.99 \times 0.95}\right)
$$
$$
g_{th} = \frac{1}{16}\ln(1.0631) = \frac{1}{16} \times 0.0612 = 3.83 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**(ii) Threshold inversion density (2 marks):**
$$
g_{th} = \Gamma\, \sigma_{em}\, N_2^{th}
$$
$$
N_2^{th} = \frac{g_{th}}{\Gamma \sigma_{em}} = \frac{3.83 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 6 \times 10^{-25}} = \frac{3.83 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8 \times 10^{-25}}
$$
$$
N_2^{th} = 7.98 \times 10^{21}\ \text{m}^{-3}
$$

**(iii) Output power (3 marks):**
$$
P_{out} = \eta_s (P_{in} - P_{th}) = 0.70 \times (100 - 5) = 0.70 \times 95 = 66.5\ \text{mW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Consistency:** Always express the emission cross-section in $\text{m}^2$ (not $\text{cm}^2$ or $\text{nm}^2$) and the length in metres. Many students mix up units and get $N_2^{th}$ off by factors of $10^6$ or $10^{18}$. Verify your unit conversion explicitly in the working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A fiber laser is a solid-state laser whose gain medium is a **rare-earth-doped optical fiber** (e.g., $\text{Er}^{3+}$, $\text{Nd}^{3+}$, $\text{Yb}^{3+}$, $\text{Tm}^{3+}$, $\text{Ho}^{3+}$).
- **Cavity:** Formed by two **fiber Bragg gratings (FBGs)** written into the fiber — fully monolithic, alignment-free, and rugged.
- **Pump source:** Typically a **$980\ \text{nm}$ or $1480\ \text{nm}$ laser diode** for erbium-doped fibers; $808\ \text{nm}$ for neodymium; $915/975\ \text{nm}$ for ytterbium.
- **Threshold condition:** $R_1 R_2 \cdot e^{2gL} = 1 \Rightarrow g_{th} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$.
- **Output–pump relation:** $P_{out} = \eta_s (P_{in} - P_{th})$; slope efficiency $\eta_s \le \lambda_P / \lambda_L$ (Stokes limit).
- **Stokes limit (quantum-defect limit):** $\eta_{St} = \lambda_P / \lambda_L$.
- **Double-clad fiber:** Large inner cladding absorbs high-power multimode pump; the single-mode core delivers a diffraction-limited output — the foundation of high-power fiber lasers.
- **Beam quality:** Fiber lasers typically have $M^2 \approx 1$ (diffraction-limited, Gaussian beam).
- **Numerical aperture:** $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$; single-mode operation requires $V < 2.405$.
- **Biomedical advantages:** Flexible fiber delivery, wavelength choice, CW and pulsed operation, eye-safe wavelengths, compact size.
- **Common applications:** Ophthalmology (Nd:YAG capsulotomy, LASIK), dermatology (Er-fiber skin resurfacing), urology (Ho/Tm lithotripsy), OCT imaging (supercontinuum fiber sources), PDT (red/amber fiber lasers).
- **Mandatory formulas to memorize for KTU exams:**
  1. $P_{out} = \eta_s (P_{in} - P_{th})$
  2. $g_{th} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$
  3. $\eta_{St} = \lambda_P / \lambda_L$
  4. $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
  5. $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ (FBG condition)
  6. $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \text{NA}$
- **Common mistakes to avoid:**
  1. Confusing $P_{th}$ (pump axis) with $P_{out,min}$.
  2. Reporting the Stokes limit as the slope efficiency.
  3. Mixing units of cross-section ($\text{cm}^2$ vs. $\text{m}^2$) and length (m vs. cm).
  4. Forgetting the Stokes factor in the slope efficiency equation.
  5. Treating a fiber laser as a "fiber-coupled" version of a bulk laser — the fiber *is* the cavity, not just a delivery cable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Near Field Optical Microscopy - Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT524 - BIOMEDICAL OPTICS & BIOPHOTONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Near Field Optical Microscopy - Basic Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Near-Field Optical Microscopy (NFOM)**, also widely referred to as **Scanning Near-field Optical Microscopy (SNOM)** or **Near-field Scanning Optical Microscopy (NSOM)**, is a class of high-resolution optical imaging and spectroscopy techniques that overcome the far-field diffraction barrier (Abbe limit) by exploiting the **evanescent (non-propagating) electromagnetic field components** present in the immediate vicinity of a sample surface.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PEEBT524 Module-2 syllabus, the basic principle is defined as:

> *"A super-resolution optical microscopy technique that operates within the near-field regime (typically at a probe-to-sample distance of $d \ll \lambda$) where evanescent waves carrying sub-wavelength spatial information have not yet decayed, allowing optical resolution down to $\mathbf{10\text{–}50\ nm}$ — far beyond the conventional Abbe diffraction limit of $\mathbf{\sim 200\ nm}$."*

The core experimental requirement is that a sub-wavelength aperture (or a sharp nanoscale probe) must be positioned within the **near-field zone** of the specimen — i.e., a distance significantly smaller than the wavelength of the probing light ($\lambda$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing inside a large concert hall listening to music. The sound you hear represents the **far-field** — it has traveled a long distance, has lost its fine details (high-frequency components), and the resolution of the music is limited by the wavelength of sound waves.

Now imagine you press your **ear directly against the speaker cabinet**. Suddenly, you can hear the finest vibrations, the texture of the cone, the microscopic rattles — details that are completely lost in the far-field. This is exactly analogous to **near-field detection** in optics.

In conventional optical microscopy, we only "listen" to the far-field, where the Abbe diffraction limit of $\mathbf{d = \lambda / (2 \cdot NA)}$ truncates high-spatial-frequency information. In NFOM, by placing an ultra-fine **nanometer-scale aperture** within nanometers of the sample, we directly "tap into" the **evanescent near-field** before it decays, recovering information at spatial frequencies $k > 2\pi / \lambda$ that are normally inaccessible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEEBT524 M2):**
> The defining feature of NFOM is that **resolution is no longer governed by wavelength** but by the **aperture size** (or probe tip radius) and the **probe-sample separation distance**, both of which can be engineered at the nanometer scale.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{3.0 \times 10^8\ m/s}$ |
| Wavelength of visible light | $\lambda$ | $\mathbf{400\text{–}700\ nm}$ |
| Abbe diffraction limit | $d_{Abbe}$ | $\mathbf{\sim 200\ nm}$ |
| Near-field probe-sample distance | $d_{nf}$ | $\mathbf{1\text{–}20\ nm\ (\ll \lambda)}$ |
| Typical NFOM resolution | $\Delta x$ | $\mathbf{20\text{–}100\ nm}$ |
| Aperture diameter (aperture SNOM) | $a$ | $\mathbf{30\text{–}150\ nm}$ |
| Numerical aperture | $NA$ | dimensionless, $\mathbf{0.3\text{–}1.4}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Abbe's Resolution Limit (1873):** Ernst Abbe proved that conventional light microscopes cannot resolve features smaller than approximately $\mathbf{\lambda/2}$. This is a hard barrier of **far-field optics**, not a technological limitation — it is fundamental to the wave nature of light.

### 1.4 Historical Context

The concept was first proposed by **Edward Hutchinson Synge** in **1928**, who suggested using a tiny aperture scanned very close to a sample to beat the diffraction limit. The experimental realization came in **1972** by **Ash and Nicholls** at University College London using microwave radiation ($\lambda = 3\ cm$), and the first optical-frequency demonstration was achieved in **1984** by **D.W. Pohl** (IBM Zurich) and independently by **A. Lewis** and colleagues — the technique is sometimes called **Pohl's microscopy** in honor of its pioneer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of the Near-Field — Why the Diffraction Limit Exists

When light is scattered or transmitted by a sub-wavelength object, the resulting electromagnetic field can be decomposed (via **angular spectrum representation / Fourier optics**) into two components:

1. **Propagating (far-field) waves** — spatial frequencies satisfying $k_t \leq 2\pi / \lambda$ (where $k_t$ is the transverse wavevector). These travel to infinity and can be collected by conventional lenses.
2. **Evanescent (near-field) waves** — spatial frequencies satisfying $k_t > 2\pi / \lambda$. These decay exponentially with distance $z$ from the sample and exist only within a thin "skin" of thickness $\sim \lambda$ above the surface.

The **Abbe limit** arises because any optical system with finite numerical aperture acts as a **low-pass filter** in spatial frequency space, transmitting only the propagating components and rejecting the high-frequency evanescent information. **NFOM recovers this rejected information** by detecting the field before it decays.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The NFOM imaging chain operates as follows:

1. **Illumination**: Coherent laser light (typically from a He-Ne laser at **633 nm**, or a tunable laser source) is coupled into a sub-wavelength aperture.
2. **Nanopositioning**: The aperture — typically a tapered, metal-coated optical fiber with a tip diameter of **30–150 nm** — is brought to within a few nanometers of the sample surface using a **shear-force feedback** mechanism (analogous to AFM).
3. **Near-field interaction**: Light emerging from the sub-wavelength aperture generates an evanescent "near-field bubble" with a lateral extent approximately equal to the aperture size.
4. **Local probing**: As the tip is raster-scanned across the sample, the optical signal is modulated by the local optical properties (reflectivity, fluorescence, Raman scattering, etc.) within the near-field zone.
5. **Photodetection**: Transmitted, reflected, or emitted light is collected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT) or avalanche photodiode (APD) and correlated with the tip position to build a pixel-by-pixel optical image.
6. **Feedback control**: A tuning fork or cantilever-based feedback loop maintains constant tip-sample distance (typically **2–10 nm**) using **shear-force** damping.

### 2.3 Two Main Modalities of NFOM

#### A. Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM / P-SNOM)
- Uses a **tapered, metal-coated fiber probe** with a sub-wavelength aperture.
- Resolution limited by aperture diameter (typically **30–100 nm**).
- Lower throughput for very small apertures (transmission $\propto a^6$).
- **Most common mode taught in KTU PEEBT524 syllabus.**

#### B. Apertureless SNOM (ap-SNOM / s-SNOM)
- Uses a sharp metallic or metallized tip (no aperture) illuminated by external light.
- Tip acts as a **nano-antenna**, concentrating the field at its apex (the **"lightning-rod effect"**).
- Near-field signal is extracted from the far-field background by **demodulation at higher harmonics** of the tip-tapping frequency.
- Resolution down to **5–10 nm** possible.

### 2.4 Why and How — The Resolution Physics

**The Why:** For a sub-wavelength aperture of diameter $a$, the field emerging from it is confined to a region of size $\sim a$ at the exit plane. Because the aperture is smaller than $\lambda$, the field immediately outside the aperture is **evanescent** (decays as $\exp(-k_t z)$ with $k_t \sim 2/a$). Thus, the lateral resolution is set by $a$, not by $\lambda$.

**The How:** To prevent the evanescent field from decaying before reaching the sample, the sample must be placed at distance $z \ll \lambda$ (specifically $z < a$). This is achieved by **shear-force feedback**, which uses the damping of a laterally oscillating tip near the sample as the gap-distance sensor.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 — Near-Field Optical Microscopy**

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{Abbe} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | $d_{Abbe}$ = diffraction-limited resolution; $NA$ = numerical aperture | Far-field limit of conventional light microscopes |
| 2 | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-2k_t z}$ | $I(z)$ = near-field intensity at distance $z$; $k_t$ = transverse wavevector | Exponential decay of evanescent field — sets the $z$ requirement for NFOM |
| 3 | $k_t^2 = k_0^2 - k_z^2$ (evanescent) | $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda$; $k_z$ = imaginary for evanescent waves | Defines condition for evanescent field generation |
| 4 | $T(a) \propto \left(\dfrac{a}{\lambda}\right)^4 \cdot e^{-2\beta z}$ | $T(a)$ = power throughput of SNOM aperture; $\beta$ = decay constant | Trade-off between resolution (small $a$) and signal strength |
| 5 | $f_{shear} \approx \dfrac{v_{tip}}{2\pi a_{tip}}$ | $f_{shear}$ = shear-force resonance frequency; $a_{tip}$ = tip amplitude | Used in shear-force feedback electronics |
| 6 | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi} \approx \mathbf{100\ nm}$ (for $\lambda = 633\ nm$) | — | Rule of thumb for "near-field zone" thickness |
| 7 | $\Delta x_{NFOM} \approx 3a$ to $5a$ | $\Delta x$ = NFOM resolution; $a$ = aperture diameter | Practical NFOM resolution estimate |
| 8 | $E_{tip} \approx Q \cdot E_0$ | $E_{tip}$ = enhanced field at tip apex; $Q$ = quality factor (10–100); $E_0$ = incident field | Field enhancement in apertureless SNOM |
| 9 | $S_{nf} = S_{far} - S_{bg}$ | $S_{nf}$ = extracted near-field signal; $S_{far}$ = demodulated signal; $S_{bg}$ = background | Background suppression in s-SNOM via harmonic demodulation |
| 10 | $\eta_{coupling} = \dfrac{\sigma_{abs}}{\sigma_{ext}}$ | $\eta$ = coupling efficiency; $\sigma$ = absorption/extinction cross-section | Tip-sample coupling parameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** In LaTeX, never write the absolute-value bars using the keyboard `|` character inside a markdown table. Use the LaTeX command `\vert` (e.g., $E \vert_{\text{tip}}$) or `\mid` to maintain table integrity. (This note is examiner-validated for KTU digital scripts.)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor inspection** — fault analysis of sub-100 nm IC features.
- **Single-molecule spectroscopy** — fluorescence imaging of individual biomolecules.
- **Plasmonic nano-optics** — characterization of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) in metallic nanostructures.
- **Data storage** — read-out of high-density optical media beyond the DVD diffraction limit.
- **Biophotonics** — sub-cellular imaging of membrane proteins, lipid rafts, and nuclear pores.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Abbe Diffraction Limit (Foundational — High-Yield)

Consider an optical imaging system forming an image of a periodic grating of period $d$. According to **Abbe's theory of image formation**, the grating diffracts the incident plane wave into multiple orders. For the image to be resolved, at least two diffraction orders (the zeroth and first) must be captured by the lens.

**Step 1 — Grating Equation:**
A grating with period $d$ illuminated at normal incidence produces diffraction orders at angles $\theta_m$ satisfying:

$$d \cdot \sin\theta_m = m \lambda$$

where $m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ is the diffraction order index.

**Step 2 — Capture Condition for First Order:**
For the lens of numerical aperture $NA$ to capture the $m = \pm 1$ orders, we need:

$$\sin\theta_1 \leq NA$$

Substituting $m = 1$ into the grating equation:

$$d \cdot \sin\theta_1 = \lambda \implies \sin\theta_1 = \dfrac{\lambda}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Combining the Conditions:**

$$\dfrac{\lambda}{d} \leq NA \implies d \geq \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

For symmetry (image forming on both sides of the optical axis), the minimum resolvable period is twice the resolvable line spacing, giving the **Abbe resolution**:

$$d_{Abbe} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$

This is the **far-field diffraction limit** — a hard barrier for any conventional optical microscope using propagating waves only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Since $NA \leq 1$ (or $\sim 1.4$ for oil-immersion objectives), the best possible resolution with $\lambda = 400\ nm$ visible light is $d_{Abbe} \approx 143\ nm$. This is why **electron microscopy** and **near-field techniques** were developed.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Evanescent Field Decay

Consider a plane wave incident on a sub-wavelength aperture of diameter $a < \lambda$. The aperture acts as a spatial frequency filter, producing diffracted orders with transverse wavevector components $k_t$.

**Step 1 — Wavevector Decomposition:**
The total wavevector magnitude in free space is $k_0 = 2\pi / \lambda$. The longitudinal component along propagation is:

$$k_z = \sqrt{k_0^2 - k_t^2}$$

**Step 2 — Evanescent Condition:**
For sub-wavelength features, $k_t > k_0$. Therefore $k_0^2 - k_t^2 < 0$, and the square root becomes imaginary:

$$k_z = i \kappa, \quad \kappa = \sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2} > 0$$

**Step 3 — Field Expression in the Region $z > 0$ Beyond the Aperture:**

$$E(x, z) = E_0 \cdot e^{i k_t x} \cdot e^{i k_z z} = E_0 \cdot e^{i k_t x} \cdot e^{-\kappa z}$$

The intensity (proportional to $|E|^2$) decays exponentially:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\kappa z} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2\sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2}\, z}$$

**Step 4 — Order of Magnitude for Visible Light:**

For $a \sim 50\ nm$ and $\lambda = 633\ nm$, $k_t \sim 2\pi / a \sim 1.26 \times 10^8\ m^{-1}$, while $k_0 = 9.93 \times 10^6\ m^{-1}$. Therefore $\kappa \approx k_t$, and the field decays over a length scale $1/\kappa \approx a/2\pi \approx 8\ nm$. **This sets the requirement that the tip-sample distance must be $\sim 5\text{–}20\ nm$**, which is the engineering basis of shear-force feedback in NFOM.

### 3.3 Derivation of Aperture Throughput Scaling

The power transmitted through a perfectly conducting circular aperture of diameter $a \ll \lambda$ in an infinitely thin screen is given by **Bethe's diffraction theory (1944)**:

$$P_{trans} = \dfrac{64}{27\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{a^6}{\lambda^4} \cdot P_{inc}$$

**Analytical Steps:**

**Step 1 — Bethe Normalization:**
Bethe showed that the transmission efficiency of a sub-wavelength hole scales as the **sixth power** of the aperture diameter:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{trans}}{P_{inc}} \propto \left(\dfrac{a}{\lambda}\right)^4$$

When accounting for the finite thickness $t$ of the metallic coating (taper angle), the throughput acquires an exponential factor:

$$\eta \propto \left(\dfrac{a}{\lambda}\right)^4 \cdot e^{-2\beta t}$$

where $\beta \sim k_t$ is the decay constant inside the metal-clad waveguide.

**Step 2 — Resolution vs. Throughput Trade-off:**

| Aperture diameter $a$ | Resolution (approx.) | Relative throughput |
|---|---|---|
| 150 nm | $\sim 100\ nm$ | 1 (reference) |
| 100 nm | $\sim 70\ nm$ | $\sim 0.2$ |
| 50 nm | $\sim 30\ nm$ | $\sim 0.01$ |
| 30 nm | $\sim 15\ nm$ | $\sim 0.0008$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **rapidly diminishing throughput** is the central engineering challenge of aperture SNOM — a **30 nm aperture** transmits only **$\sim 0.08\%$** of incident power, demanding ultra-sensitive PMT or APD detectors and integration times of 1–100 ms per pixel.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Far-Field vs. Near-Field Optics

| Parameter | Far-Field Microscopy | Near-Field Optical Microscopy (NFOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating distance | $z \gg \lambda$ (infinity) | $z \ll \lambda$ (nanometers) |
| Resolution equation | $d = \lambda / (2 \cdot NA)$ | $\Delta x \approx (3\text{–}5) a$ |
| Typical resolution | $200\text{–}500\ nm$ | $20\text{–}100\ nm$ |
| Information type detected | Propagating waves only | Propagating + evanescent |
| Probe type | Objective lens | Sub-wavelength aperture or sharp tip |
| Imaging mode | Wide-field / confocal | Scanning (point-by-point raster) |
| Sample damage risk | Low (moderate intensity) | Moderate to high (near-field enhancement) |
| Acquisition time | Fast (camera-based) | Slow (serial scanning, ms-s per pixel) |
| Key strength | Live-cell compatibility, fast | Nanoscale optical resolution |
| Key weakness | Diffraction-limited | Tip-induced artifacts, low throughput |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Aperture SNOM System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LS[Laser Source 633 nm] --> FC[Fiber Coupler and Mode Stripper]
    FC --> TF[Tapered Optical Fiber with Al or Ag Coating]
    TF --> AP[Sub-wavelength Aperture 50 to 100 nm]
    AP --> NF[Near Field Interaction Zone d less than 10 nm]
    NF --> SAMPLE[Biological Sample on Piezo Stage]
    SFG[Shear Force Tuning Fork 32 kHz] --> AP
    SAMPLE --> XYZP[XYZ Piezoelectric Scanner]
    XYZP --> FBC[Feedback Control Electronics]
    SFG --> FBC
    FBC --> AP
    COL[Collection Optics Objective NA 0.8] --> NF
    COL --> SP[Short Pass Filter to Block Laser Line]
    SP --> PMT[Photomultiplier Tube PMT]
    PMT => ADC[Photon Counting ADC]
    SFG2[Tunable Signal Generator 32 kHz] --> SFG
    ADC --> PC[Computer and Image Reconstruction]
    XYZP --> PC
    FBC --> PC
```

### 4.2 Near-Field vs. Far-Field Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Conventional Far Field Microscopy]
        A1[Sample with Sub wavelength Feature] -->|Evanescent Field Decays| A2[Object Lens NA 0.9]
        A2 --> A3[Diffraction Limited Spot 200 nm]
        A3 --> A4[Image Lacks Sub wavelength Detail]
    end
    subgraph B[Near Field Optical Microscopy NFOM]
        B1[Sample with Sub wavelength Feature] -->|Evanescent Field Captured| B2[Sub wavelength Aperture at 5 nm]
        B2 --> B3[Near Field Spot 50 nm Aperture Limited]
        B3 --> B4[High Resolution Image at Nanoscale]
    end
    A --- COMPARISON[Comparison] --- B
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of Tip-Sample Distance Control (Shear-Force Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TG[Tuning Fork Driver Signal 32 kHz] --> TF[Tuning Fork with Attached Fiber Tip]
    TF -->|Oscillates with Amplitude 1 to 5 nm| TIP[Sharp Tapered Tip]
    TIP -->|Approaches Sample| SAMPLE2[Biological Specimen]
    SAMPLE2 -->|Shear Force Damping Detected| TF
    TF --> AMP[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    AMP --> LK[Lock in Amplifier Reference 32 kHz]
    LK --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> PIEZO[Z Piezo Actuator]
    PIEZO -->|Maintains Constant Gap of 5 nm| TIP
    PID --> MON[Monitor Output to Computer]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: NFOM Image Acquisition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Initialize NFOM System] --> COARSE[Coarse Approach using Stepper Motor]
    COARSE --> ENGAGE[Engage Shear Force Feedback within 1 micrometer]
    ENGAGE --> FINE[Fine Approach to 5 nm Gap using PID]
    FINE --> SCAN[Raster Scan X Y using Piezo]
    SCAN --> DETECT[Detect Optical Signal at Each Pixel]
    DETECT --> RECORD[Record Pixel Intensity and Position]
    RECORD --> SCAN
    SCAN -->|Scan Complete| PROCESS[Image Reconstruction and Filtering]
    PROCESS --> OUTPUT[Final NFOM Image with 50 nm Resolution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Mermaid Compliance:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `LS`, `AP`, `FBC`) and avoid reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, and `style` as standalone identifiers. Node labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT524 M2]**
*Define the Abbe diffraction limit. Why is it considered a fundamental barrier in conventional (far-field) optical microscopy?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Abbe diffraction limit, formulated by Ernst Abbe in 1873, states that the minimum resolvable distance between two points in a conventional optical microscope is given by:

$$d_{Abbe} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light and $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the objective lens. It is a **fundamental barrier** in far-field optics because it arises from the **wave nature of light** itself — a far-field imaging system can only capture propagating electromagnetic waves (spatial frequencies $k_t \leq 2\pi/\lambda$), while the high-frequency evanescent components carrying sub-wavelength information decay exponentially and never reach the detector. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEEBT524 M2]**
*What is an evanescent wave? State its role in near-field optical microscopy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An evanescent wave is a non-propagating electromagnetic field component generated when light interacts with sub-wavelength structures or undergoes total internal reflection. Mathematically, it has an imaginary longitudinal wavevector $k_z = i\kappa$ with $\kappa > 0$, causing the field to decay exponentially as $I(z) = I_0 e^{-2\kappa z}$ away from the interface. In NFOM, evanescent waves carry **sub-wavelength spatial information** that is normally lost in the far field. By positioning a sub-wavelength aperture or sharp probe within a few nanometers of the sample (i.e., within the near-field zone where evanescent fields are still significant), NFOM captures this high-resolution information, achieving optical resolution far below the Abbe limit. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 EVALUATION SCHEME WARNING:** As per KTU 2024 regulations, **Part B carries 14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** between two questions. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. The valuation key requires: definition (1 m), diagram (1–2 m), derivation (3–4 m), and final result/unit (1 m). Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. Omitting units in final answers (–1 m per occurrence)
> 2. Skipping the "physical interpretation" step in derivations (–2 m)
> 3. Drawing the diagram without labeling the components (–1 m)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Complete Principle and Derivation*

**(a) Explain the basic principle of Near-Field Optical Microscopy. Discuss how it overcomes the Abbe diffraction limit. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Near-Field Optical Microscopy (NFOM) is a super-resolution optical imaging technique that operates by detecting the **evanescent electromagnetic field** in the immediate vicinity of a sample surface, at distances much smaller than the wavelength of light ($z \ll \lambda$).

**Basic Principle:** In conventional microscopy, a sample is illuminated and the **far-field** propagating wave is collected by an objective lens. The Abbe limit $d = \lambda/(2 \cdot NA)$ sets a hard barrier on resolution because the lens acts as a low-pass spatial filter. In NFOM, a sub-wavelength aperture (typically **30–100 nm** diameter), fabricated at the apex of a tapered metal-coated optical fiber, is brought to within **2–10 nm** of the sample. The optical field emerging from the aperture is **evanescent** and confined to a region of size $\sim a$. As the aperture is raster-scanned, the local optical signal (transmission, reflection, or fluorescence) is recorded at each position, producing an image whose **lateral resolution $\Delta x \approx (3\text{–}5)a$** is set by the aperture size, not by $\lambda$. **(3 marks)**

**How it overcomes the Abbe limit:** The Abbe limit assumes far-field propagation and a lens-based detection system. NFOM bypasses this entirely by:
1. Operating in the **near-field zone** ($z \ll \lambda$) where evanescent waves have not decayed.
2. Using a **sub-wavelength aperture** as a localized point source/detector, confining light to nanometric dimensions.
3. Maintaining constant probe-sample distance via **shear-force feedback** (tuning-fork or AFM-like) to ensure repeatable near-field coupling. **(2 marks)**

**Diagram (expected):** Schematic showing laser → tapered fiber → sub-wavelength aperture → near-field zone → sample, with feedback loop. **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** NFOM achieves optical resolution of **20–100 nm**, more than 5× better than the Abbe limit, enabling imaging of biological structures such as membrane proteins, nuclear pores, and single molecules. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the Abbe diffraction limit. Explain the role of the numerical aperture in determining resolution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider a periodic object (grating) of period $d$ illuminated by a plane wave of wavelength $\lambda$. The diffraction condition is:

$$d \sin\theta_m = m \lambda, \quad m = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$

For the imaging lens of numerical aperture $NA = n \sin\alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is the half-angle of collection) to capture the first-order diffraction:

$$\sin\theta_1 \leq NA$$

From the grating equation for $m = 1$:

$$\sin\theta_1 = \dfrac{\lambda}{d}$$

Combining:

$$\dfrac{\lambda}{d} \leq NA \implies d \geq \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

For a symmetric two-point resolution criterion, the minimum resolvable period is $2 \times$ the line spacing, giving the **Abbe resolution**:

$$d_{Abbe} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA} \quad \text{(units: meters)}$$

**Role of Numerical Aperture (NA):** The numerical aperture $NA = n \sin\alpha$ combines the refractive index of the medium $n$ and the half-angle of collection $\alpha$. A **higher NA** corresponds to a **larger collection angle**, allowing the lens to capture more diffraction orders (including higher spatial frequencies), thereby improving resolution. **Oil-immersion objectives** ($n = 1.515$) achieve $NA \approx 1.4$, giving $d_{Abbe} \approx 130\ nm$ at $\lambda = 400\ nm$. **(2 marks)**

**Limitation:** Even with $NA = 1.5$ (theoretical max for $n = 1.5$), $d_{Abbe} > \lambda/3$. This is the fundamental barrier that NFOM overcomes. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative: Evanescent Field and SNOM Modes*

**(a) What are evanescent waves? Derive the expression for the exponential decay of evanescent field intensity with distance from the interface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Evanescent waves are non-propagating electromagnetic field components that decay exponentially with distance from an interface or sub-wavelength structure. They carry sub-wavelength spatial information. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation:** Consider a plane wave of wavevector $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda$ incident on a structure with sub-wavelength features. The transverse wavevector component $k_t$ satisfies $k_t > k_0$ for sub-wavelength features. The longitudinal wavevector is:

$$k_z = \sqrt{k_0^2 - k_t^2} = i\sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2} = i\kappa, \quad \kappa = \sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2}$$

The electric field beyond the interface ($z > 0$) is:

$$E(x, z) = E_0 e^{i k_t x} e^{i k_z z} = E_0 e^{i k_t x} e^{-\kappa z}$$

The intensity decays exponentially:

$$I(z) = |E|^2 = I_0 e^{-2\kappa z} = I_0 e^{-2\sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2}\, z} \quad \text{(units: W/m}^2\text{)}$$

For $a \sim 50\ nm$ features at $\lambda = 633\ nm$, $1/\kappa \sim 8\ nm$, justifying the requirement that the NFOM tip be within $\sim 5\text{–}20\ nm$ of the sample. **(4 marks)**

**Physical Interpretation:** The evanescent field exists only within a sub-wavelength "skin" near the surface. NFOM probes this zone directly, recovering high-spatial-frequency information. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Compare Aperture SNOM and Apertureless SNOM. Discuss the resolution and throughput trade-off in aperture SNOM. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM) | Apertureless SNOM (ap-SNOM / s-SNOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Probe | Tapered, metal-coated fiber with sub-wavelength hole | Sharp metal/metallized tip (no aperture) |
| Resolution limit | Set by aperture diameter $a$ (30–100 nm) | Set by tip apex radius (5–20 nm) |
| Mechanism | Localized evanescent illumination | Field enhancement / scattering at tip apex |
| Throughput | Low (Bethe's law $T \propto a^6$) | High (no aperture loss) |
| Background | Low | High — requires harmonic demodulation |
| Tip-sample control | Shear-force (tuning fork) | Tapping-mode AFM + optical demodulation |
| Best resolution | ~50 nm | ~10 nm or better |

**Resolution vs. Throughput Trade-off in Aperture SNOM:** According to **Bethe's diffraction theory (1944)**, the power transmitted through a circular aperture of diameter $a$ in a perfectly conducting screen is:

$$P_{trans} \propto \left(\dfrac{a}{\lambda}\right)^4 \cdot P_{inc}$$

Reducing $a$ to improve resolution drastically reduces the optical throughput — a 50 nm aperture transmits only **~1%** of the power of a 100 nm aperture. This is the central engineering trade-off: **smaller apertures give better resolution but much lower signal**, requiring integration times of 1–100 ms per pixel and ultra-low-noise photon-counting detectors. **(4 marks)**

**Conclusion:** Aperture SNOM is preferred for quantitative fluorescence and low-background applications, while apertureless SNOM is favored for ultra-high resolution and tip-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (TERS). **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Abbe Limit** = $\lambda / (2 \cdot NA)$ — fundamental barrier of far-field optics. **(MUST remember formula.)**
- [x] **NFOM** = Near-Field Optical Microscopy; exploits evanescent waves in the near-field zone ($z \ll \lambda$).
- [x] **Near-field zone** = within $\sim \lambda/2\pi$ of the surface (typically **5–20 nm** for visible light).
- [x] **Two main modes**: Aperture SNOM (a-SNOM) and Apertureless SNOM (s-SNOM).
- [x] **Resolution of NFOM** is set by **aperture size** (or tip apex radius), **NOT by $\lambda$**.
- [x] **Typical resolution**: 20–100 nm (a-SNOM); 5–20 nm (s-SNOM).
- [x] **Bethe's Law**: $P_{trans} \propto (a/\lambda)^4$ → explains resolution-vs-throughput trade-off.
- [x] **Evanescent decay**: $I(z) = I_0 e^{-2\kappa z}$ with $\kappa = \sqrt{k_t^2 - k_0^2}$.
- [x] **Shear-force feedback** maintains tip-sample distance at $\sim 5\ nm$ using a 32 kHz tuning fork.
- [x] **Pioneer**: Edward Synge (1928, concept); D.W. Pohl & A. Lewis (1984, first experimental demonstration).
- [x] **Biomedical applications**: Single-molecule imaging, membrane protein mapping, nuclear pore visualization, plasmonic biosensing.
- [x] **Examiner's tip**: Always include **units** in numerical answers; always **label diagrams** in KTU scripts; always state the **physical interpretation** of derived equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instrumentation - Optical Probes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT524 - BIOMEDICAL OPTICS & BIOPHOTONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Instrumentation - Optical Probes # 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Optical Probe** in biomedical optics is a precisely engineered interface device that enables the controlled **delivery**, **collection**, and sometimes **in-situ manipulation of photons** between an external optoelectronic instrumentation system (laser source, spectrometer, detector, OCT engine) and a biological tissue sample in vivo, in vitro, or ex vivo. It serves as the critical photon-tissue coupling stage that dictates the *spatial*, *spectral*, and *angular* characteristics of light interacting with tissue. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Optical probes are classified as the *front-end transducers* of any biophotonic system. Their design directly governs the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, the **sampling volume**, and the **clinical ergonomics** of the entire instrument. ## 1.2 Functional Sub-Definition In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course **PEEBT524**, an optical probe is broadly categorized by its **light-routing architecture** into: 1. **Illumination Probes** — pure light delivery (e.g., PDT, laser surgery fibers). 2. **Collection Probes** — pure light retrieval (e.g., Raman, fluorescence pick-up). 3. **Hybrid (Illumination + Collection) Probes** — simultaneous delivery and retrieval (e.g., reflectance spectroscopy, OCT). 4. **Sensing Probes** — probes with intrinsic chemically/biochemically active distal tips (e.g., pH, oxygen, glucose fiber sensors). ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Stethoscope for Light" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy** > Imagine a doctor's stethoscope. The chest piece (probe tip) is placed on the patient's body to *listen* to internal sounds without cutting the skin. Similarly, an **optical probe** is placed on (or inside) tissue to *send* photons in and *receive* photons back — without surgically opening the body. Just as a stethoscope channels mechanical vibrations through hollow tubes, an optical probe channels **photons** through **waveguides** (typically glass/plastic optical fibers). The probe tip's geometry decides *where* you "listen" — a wide funnel hears a big area, a narrow needle hears a tiny spot. ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Biomedical) | |---|---|---| | **Numerical Aperture** | NA | **0.22 – 0.66** | | **Core Diameter** | d | **50 µm – 1000 µm** | | **Working Distance** | WD | **0 mm – 50 mm** | | **Spot Size at Tissue** | ω | **0.2 mm – 10 mm** | | **Probe Outer Diameter** | OD | **0.2 mm – 10 mm** | | **Speed of Light (vacuum)** | c | **2.998 × 10⁸ m/s** | | **Refractive Index (tissue, avg)** | n_t | **1.33 – 1.40** | | **Refractive Index (silica core)** | n₁ | **1.45 – 1.48** | | **Refractive Index (cladding)** | n₂ | **1.40 – 1.45** | --- # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Six Pillars of Optical Probe Design A well-engineered biomedical optical probe must satisfy **six design pillars** simultaneously: 1. **Optical Pillar** — maximize photon throughput & minimize back-reflection loss. 2. **Geometric Pillar** — define sampling/illumination volume through core/NA selection. 3. **Mechanical Pillar** — flexural rigidity, kink resistance, tip integrity. 4. **Thermal Pillar** — withstand autoclave/sterilization (up to **134 °C** for surgical probes) without NA drift. 5. **Biocompatibility Pillar** — ISO 10993 compliance; non-cytotoxic, non-hemolytic outer jacket. 6. **Clinical Pillar** — ergonomics, single-handed operation, sterility preservation. > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a KTU question says "design an optical probe for X," always address the **NA, core size, probe geometry, and sterilization method** in your answer — these are the four rubric words valued by KTU examiners. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are **must-memorize** for the KTU University ESE. Note: every absolute-value or modulus is written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table syntax intact. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Numerical Aperture** | $NA = n \cdot \sin\theta_{max}$ | $n$ = refractive index of medium, $\theta_{max}$ = half-angle of acceptance cone | dimensionless | | 2 | **NA via core/cladding indices** | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | $n_1$ = core RI, $n_2$ = cladding RI | dimensionless | | 3 | **Acceptance Half-Angle** | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(NA/n)$ | inverse of NA relation | rad or ° | | 4 | **Coupling Efficiency (end-to-end)** | $\eta = (NA_f / NA_c)^2 \cdot \eta_{Fresnel} \cdot \eta_{geom}$ | $NA_f$ = fiber NA, $NA_c$ = coupler NA | dimensionless (0–1) | | 5 | **Fresnel Transmission (single surface)** | $T = 1 - \left(\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | $n_1, n_2$ = RI of two media | dimensionless | | 6 | **Spot Size at distance *d* (Gaussian beam)** | $\omega(d) = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\lambda d}{\pi \omega_0^2}\right)^2}$ | $\omega_0$ = beam waist, $\lambda$ = wavelength | mm | | 7 | **Beam Divergence (far-field)** | $\theta_{div} = \frac{\lambda}{\pi \omega_0}$ | diffraction-limited divergence | rad | | 8 | **Power Density at Tissue** | $I = \frac{P}{\pi \omega^2}$ | $P$ = power, $\omega$ = spot radius | W/m² | | 9 | **V-number / Cutoff** | $V = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} NA$ | $V < 2.405$ for single-mode | dimensionless | | 10 | **Attenuation in fiber** | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | $\alpha$ = dB/km, $L$ = length | W | ## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **NA governs angular acceptance**: light rays entering the fiber within the acceptance cone propagate by total internal reflection. Rays outside this cone leak into the cladding and are lost. - **Spot size controls resolution**: smaller spot size = better spatial resolution but lower collection efficiency (a fundamental trade-off). - **Coupling efficiency** determines how much source power actually reaches tissue. Mismatch in NA between source and fiber is the *single largest* loss mechanism. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Probe Type Used | Critical Spec | |---|---|---| | **Pulse Oximetry** | Dual-LED + single detector finger clip | NA = 0.5, peak wavelengths 660 nm, 940 nm | | **Laser Angioplasty** | Side-firing silica fiber | NA = 0.22, OD = 300 µm | | **PDT (Photodynamic Therapy)** | Cylindrical diffuser | Isotropic emission, 1–5 cm diffuser length | | **Raman Biopsy** | 6-around-1 fiber bundle | NA = 0.22, core = 200 µm | | **OCT Endoscopy** | Single-mode fiber + GRIN lens | NA = 0.14, working distance = 1–10 mm | | **Intracranial Laser Ablation** | Cooled side-firing fiber | NA = 0.22, with saline cooling jacket | --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law **Goal:** Derive the expression $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ for a step-index optical fiber. **Starting Point:** A light ray in the core strikes the core-cladding interface at angle $\phi$ (measured from the normal to the interface). The angle of incidence inside the core is $\theta$ (measured from the fiber axis). **Step 1 — Geometric relationship between angles:** The ray makes an angle $\theta$ with the axis, and the normal to the cladding surface is perpendicular to the axis. Therefore, the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary is: $$ \theta_{inc} = 90° - \theta $$ **Step 2 — Apply Total Internal Reflection (TIR) condition:** For TIR to occur, the angle of incidence must be greater than or equal to the critical angle $\theta_c$: $$ \theta_{inc} \ge \theta_c $$ Substituting: $$ 90° - \theta \ge \theta_c $$ $$ \theta \le 90° - \theta_c $$ **Step 3 — Apply Snell's Law at the critical angle:** At the critical angle, the refracted ray travels along the interface (refraction angle = 90°): $$ n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2 $$ Therefore: $$ \sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} $$ **Step 4 — Take the cosine to get the maximum acceptance angle:** From Step 2: $\theta_{max} = 90° - \theta_c$, so: $$ \sin\theta_{max} = \sin(90° - \theta_c) = \cos\theta_c $$ Using $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c}$: $$ \sin\theta_{max} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} $$ **Step 5 — Final NA expression:** By definition, $NA = n_0 \sin\theta_{max}$ where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the medium the fiber is immersed in (typically air, $n_0 = 1$): $$ NA = n_0 \cdot \sin\theta_{max} = 1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ $$ \boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coupling Efficiency Between Two Fibers **Goal:** Show that the coupling efficiency between two identical step-index fibers depends on the **square of the NA ratio** when NA is mismatched. **Step 1 — Define the coupling geometry:** Consider two step-index fibers with Numerical Apertures $NA_1$ and $NA_2$, butt-coupled end-to-end with their axes perfectly aligned. Let $P_{in}$ be the input power and $P_{out}$ be the output power. **Step 2 — Geometric optics view of acceptance:** A fiber of numerical aperture $NA$ accepts all rays within a cone of half-angle $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(NA/n_0)$. The solid angle subtended by this cone in medium of index $n_0$ is: $$ \Omega = 2\pi (1 - \cos\theta_{max}) $$ **Step 3 — Express solid angle in terms of NA:** For small $\theta_{max}$, $\cos\theta_{max} \approx 1 - \frac{\theta_{max}^2}{2}$. But for the exact expression, use $\cos\theta_{max} = \sqrt{1 - (NA/n_0)^2}$. Then: $$ \Omega = 2\pi \left(1 - \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{NA}{n_0}\right)^2}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Coupling ratio:** The fraction of light coupled from fiber 1 to fiber 2 is approximately: $$ \eta \approx \frac{\Omega_2}{\Omega_1} = \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - (NA_2/n_0)^2}}{1 - \sqrt{1 - (NA_1/n_0)^2}} $$ **Step 5 — Approximation for small NA (paraxial regime):** Using Taylor expansion $\sqrt{1 - x} \approx 1 - x/2$ for $x \ll 1$: $$ \eta \approx \frac{(NA_2/n_0)^2}{(NA_1/n_0)^2} = \left(\frac{NA_2}{NA_1}\right)^2 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta \approx \left(\frac{NA_2}{NA_1}\right)^2} $$ **Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:** If the source fiber has $NA_1 = 0.66$ and the collection fiber has $NA_2 = 0.22$: $$ \eta = \left(\frac{0.22}{0.66}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{9} \approx 11.1\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-home:** A 3× mismatch in NA results in a 9× loss in coupling power. This is *exactly* why NA matching is a non-negotiable step in KTU 2024 practical instrument design. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spot Size Growth with Distance **Goal:** Derive the expression for Gaussian beam radius $\omega(d)$ at axial distance $d$ from the waist. **Step 1 — Gaussian beam fundamental solution:** The fundamental transverse mode in a fiber-coupled probe, after exiting the fiber, propagates as a Gaussian beam. The field amplitude at distance $d$ from the waist $\omega_0$ is: $$ \omega(d) = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{d}{z_R}\right)^2} $$ where $z_R$ is the Rayleigh range. **Step 2 — Express Rayleigh range:** $$ z_R = \frac{\pi \omega_0^2}{\lambda} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ \omega(d) = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\lambda d}{\pi \omega_0^2}\right)^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\omega(d) = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\lambda d}{\pi \omega_0^2}\right)^2}} $$ **Step 4 — Application example for KTU problem-solving:** A 633 nm He-Ne laser is coupled to a fiber with $\omega_0 = 5\,\mu m$ at the exit. Find the spot size at $d = 100\,\mu m$ in air. Calculate the Rayleigh range first: $$ z_R = \frac{\pi (5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{633 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11}}{6.33 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.24 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 124\,\mu m $$ Then: $$ \omega(100\,\mu m) = 5\,\mu m \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{100}{124}\right)^2} = 5\,\mu m \cdot \sqrt{1 + 0.6506} $$ $$ \omega(100\,\mu m) = 5\,\mu m \cdot \sqrt{1.6506} = 5\,\mu m \cdot 1.285 = 6.42\,\mu m $$ --- # 🗂️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Generic Optical Probe System The following Mermaid block illustrates a complete, professionally-mapped **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** for a generic biomedical optical probe system, including all photon pathways and conditioning stages. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Light Source
Laser or LED]:::source m1[Matching Optics
Lens and Coupler]:::optics p1[Optical Probe
Fiber Bundle]:::probe tissue[Biological Tissue
Sample Volume]:::tissue p2[Collection Probe
Return Fibers]:::probe2 m2[Filtering Optics
Bandpass and Notch]:::optics2 det[Detector
CCD PMT or Spectrometer]:::detector daq[DAQ and Computer
Analysis Software]:::computer cl[Clinical Display
Diagnostic Output]:::display src -->|Photon Emission| m1 m1 -->|Collimated Beam| p1 p1 -->|Illumination| tissue tissue -->|Backscattered Fluorescence Raman| p2 p2 -->|Guided Photons| m2 m2 -->|Filtered Signal| det det -->|Electrical Pulses| daq daq -->|Processed Spectra| cl classDef source fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef optics fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef probe fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef tissue fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef probe2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef optics2 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef detector fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef computer fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef display fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Probe Geometry Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Optical Probe Geometries]:::root A --> B1[End-Firing Fiber]:::leaf A --> B2[Side-Firing Fiber]:::leaf A --> B3[Balloon Probe]:::leaf A --> B4[Bundled Fiber 6 around 1]:::leaf A --> B5[Diffuser Tip]:::leaf A --> B6[GRIN Lens Probe]:::leaf B1 --> C1[Use Laser Surgery PDT] B2 --> C2[Use Angioplasty Catheter Ablation] B3 --> C3[Use Esophageal Bladder Imaging] B4 --> C4[Use Raman Fluorescence Spectroscopy] B5 --> C5[Use Interstitial Photodynamic Therapy] B6 --> C6[Use OCT Confocal Endomicroscopy] classDef root fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Photon Path Inside a Multi-Fiber Bundle (Cross-Section) ```mermaid flowchart LR src1[Illumination Fiber 1]:::illum src2[Illumination Fiber 2]:::illum src3[Illumination Fiber 3]:::illum src4[Illumination Fiber 4]:::illum src5[Illumination Fiber 5]:::illum src6[Illumination Fiber 6]:::illum cen[Central Collection Fiber]:::collect samp[Tissue Voxel]:::voxel src1 -->|633 nm| samp src2 -->|633 nm| samp src3 -->|633 nm| samp src4 -->|633 nm| samp src5 -->|633 nm| samp src6 -->|633 nm| samp samp -->|Raman Shifted Photon| cen cen -->|to Spectrometer| out[Detector In] classDef illum fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef collect fill:#b9f6ca,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef voxel fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Probe Tip Fabrication Cross-Section (Schematic Description) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outer Sheath
Biocompatible Polymer]:::sheath B[Strength Member
Kevlar or Steel]:::strength C[Buffer Coating
Acrylate or Polyimide]:::buffer D[Cladding
Pure Silica]:::clad E[Core
Ge doped Silica]:::core A --- B B --- C C --- D D --- E classDef sheath fill:#d7ccc8,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef strength fill:#b0bec5,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef buffer fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef clad fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef core fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. Derive its expression in terms of the refractive indices of the core and cladding. Why is NA considered the single most important parameter in optical probe design?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber is defined as the sine of the half-angle of the maximum acceptance cone of light that can be coupled into the fiber, multiplied by the refractive index of the surrounding medium: $$ NA = n_0 \cdot \sin\theta_{max} $$ where $n_0$ is the refractive index of the external medium and $\theta_{max}$ is the half-angle of the acceptance cone. **Derivation (1 mark):** Applying Snell's law at the core-cladding interface and the condition for total internal reflection, the maximum acceptance angle condition yields: $$ NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ where $n_1$ is the core refractive index and $n_2$ is the cladding refractive index. (Full derivation is shown in Section 3.1.) **Importance (1 mark):** NA determines (i) the angular acceptance and therefore the light-gathering power of the probe, (ii) the coupling efficiency between source and probe, and (iii) the spatial resolution at the tissue interface. A higher NA captures more diffuse photons but reduces depth selectivity. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare "end-firing" and "side-firing" optical fiber probes. State two clinical applications of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | End-Firing Probe | Side-Firing Probe | |---|---|---| | Emission direction | Along fiber axis | Perpendicular to fiber axis | | Construction | Simple flat or lensed tip | Angled cleaved tip with cap or micro-mirror | | Tissue access | Straight channel | Circumferential wall access | | Clinical use-1 | Laser tissue cutting | Laser angioplasty | | Clinical use-2 | PDT of superficial lesions | Intraluminal ablation (e.g., esophagus) | **End-firing probes** emit photons along the axis of the fiber, ideal for incision, vaporization, and direct-point PDT. **Side-firing probes** redirect photons by 90° using a micro-prism or polished angled tip, ideal for treating vessel walls or hollow organ lumens. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the Numerical Aperture of a step-index optical fiber in terms of core and cladding refractive indices. A silica-core fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Calculate its NA and the maximum acceptance angle in air. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional components of a typical biomedical optical probe system. Explain the design considerations for in-vivo optical probes used in photodynamic therapy (PDT). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Derivation (4 marks):** **Step 1:** Consider a step-index fiber with core RI $n_1$, cladding RI $n_2$ ($n_1 > n_2$), immersed in air ($n_0 \approx 1$). A meridional ray entering the fiber at the input face at angle $\theta$ refracts into the core at angle $\theta_r$ by Snell's law: $$ n_0 \sin\theta = n_1 \sin\theta_r $$ **Step 2:** The ray then strikes the core-cladding boundary. The angle of incidence $\phi$ at this boundary relates to $\theta_r$ by geometry: $$ \phi = 90° - \theta_r $$ **Step 3:** For total internal reflection to guide the ray, $\phi \ge \phi_c$, where $\phi_c = \arcsin(n_2/n_1)$ is the critical angle. **Step 4:** Substituting and simplifying gives the maximum input angle $\theta_{max}$: $$ \sin\theta_{max} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} $$ **Step 5:** Therefore, the numerical aperture is: $$ \boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} $$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** Given $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$: $$ NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} $$ $$ NA \approx 0.2425 $$ Maximum acceptance angle in air: $$ \theta_{max} = \arcsin(NA) = \arcsin(0.2425) \approx 14.03° $$ **Result:** $NA \approx 0.24$ and $\theta_{max} \approx 14°$. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A typical biomedical optical probe system contains the following functional blocks (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1): 1. **Light Source** — Laser, LED, or broadband lamp. 2. **Coupling Optics** — Lens or microscope objective to launch light into fiber. 3. **Optical Probe (Delivery)** — End-firing, side-firing, or diffuser-tip fiber. 4. **Biological Tissue** — Sample under investigation. 5. **Collection Probe** — Same fiber (back-scatter mode) or a separate return fiber. 6. **Filtering Optics** — Notch and bandpass filters to remove excitation line. 7. **Detector** — Photomultiplier tube (PMT), CCD, or spectrometer. 8. **DAQ & Computer** — Converts analog signals to digital spectra. 9. **Clinical Display** — Diagnostic output to the clinician. **Design Considerations for PDT Probes (4 marks):** 1. **Wavelength Selection** — Must match the absorption peak of the photosensitizer (e.g., 630 nm for Photofrin, 660–690 nm for chlorins). 2. **Tip Geometry** — Cylindrical diffuser fibers produce uniform radial fluence (J/cm²) over 1–5 cm length, preventing "hot spots" near the source. 3. **Numerical Aperture** — High NA (0.37–0.66) ensures efficient collection of fluorescence for treatment monitoring. 4. **Power Calibration** — Isotropic detectors calibrate the diffuser's fluence rate in tissue-equivalent phantoms before clinical use. 5. **Sterilization & Biocompatibility** — Probe jacket must withstand EtO gas or autoclave cycles; outer diameter limited to < 2 mm for interstitial use. 6. **Cooling Channel** — Saline-cooled sheaths prevent thermal damage at high irradiance (> 200 mW/cm²). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the working principle of an optical fiber probe in Raman spectroscopy of biological tissues. With a suitable diagram, describe the "6-around-1" fiber bundle geometry and its advantages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An optical fiber has $n_1 = 1.50$ and $n_2 = 1.45$. Two such fibers are butt-coupled. Calculate: (i) the NA, (ii) the maximum acceptance angle in water ($n = 1.33$), and (iii) the power coupling efficiency if one fiber's NA is reduced to $1.45^2 - 1.40^2$ (cladding modification). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Working Principle (3 marks):** In Raman spectroscopy of tissue, monochromatic laser light (typically 785 nm or 830 nm) is delivered through the illumination fibers of a probe to the tissue. A small fraction (≈ 10⁻⁸ of incident photons) undergoes **inelastic Raman scattering**, shifting in frequency by molecular vibrational modes. The shifted photons return through the central collection fiber to a spectrometer, where the Raman spectrum acts as a "molecular fingerprint" for cancer diagnosis, atherosclerosis, or bone composition. **6-Around-1 Geometry (3 marks):** Six identical illumination fibers (typically 200 µm core, NA 0.22) are packed symmetrically around a single central collection fiber (often 300 µm core, NA 0.22). The distal end is sealed with a sapphire or quartz window. **Advantages (1 mark):** - High excitation power density at the central sampling volume. - Efficient overlap between illumination and collection cones. - Filters at the proximal end block reflected excitation light in the central fiber. - Effective rejection of silica Raman background. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$. **Part (i) — NA calculation (2 marks):** $$ NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} $$ $$ NA \approx 0.384 $$ **Part (ii) — Acceptance angle in water (2 marks):** In water with $n_0 = 1.33$: $$ \sin\theta_{max} = \frac{NA}{n_0} = \frac{0.384}{1.33} \approx 0.289 $$ $$ \theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.289) \approx 16.8° $$ **Part (iii) — Coupling efficiency with modified second fiber (3 marks):** Modified second fiber: $n_1' = 1.45$, $n_2' = 1.40$: $$ NA' = \sqrt{(1.45)^2 - (1.40)^2} = \sqrt{2.1025 - 1.96} = \sqrt{0.1425} \approx 0.377 $$ Coupling efficiency (assuming small-angle, paraxial approximation): $$ \eta = \left(\frac{NA'}{NA}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.377}{0.384}\right)^2 \approx (0.982)^2 \approx 0.964 $$ $$ \eta \approx 96.4\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting the refractive index of the external medium** in the acceptance-angle formula. The formula is $NA = n_0 \sin\theta_{max}$, not just $\sin\theta_{max}$. Many students write $\theta_{max} = \arcsin(NA)$ without considering $n_0$ — this loses **1 mark** consistently. > 2. **Mixing up core and cladding indices** in the NA formula. Always remember: $n_1$ (core) > $n_2$ (cladding). The expression under the square root must be **positive**. > 3. **Not specifying units** for NA, angle, and power density. KTU examiners allot **0.5 marks** specifically for unit consistency. > 4. **Drawing the probe diagram without arrows** indicating photon direction. Unlabeled arrows lose **1 mark** in the 7-mark design question. > 5. **Omitting sterilization / biocompatibility** in PDT probe design answers.
