# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Axial Resolution $\Delta z$ in OCT

The axial resolution is set by the **coherence length** of the source — i.e., the spatial extent over which a fixed-phase relationship is maintained between the reference and sample electric fields.

**Step 1 — Start with the autocorrelation of a Gaussian source spectrum.**

The spectral power density of a typical broadband SLED source is well-approximated by a Gaussian function in wavenumber $k$:

$$
S(k) = S_0 \exp\!\left[ -4 \ln 2 \left( \frac{k - k_0}{\Delta k} \right)^{\!2} \right]
$$

where $k_0$ is the central wavenumber and $\Delta k$ is the FWHM spectral width in wavenumber.

**Step 2 — The complex degree of temporal coherence $\gamma(\tau)$ is the Fourier transform of the normalized source spectrum.**

$$
\gamma(\tau) = \mathcal{F}\{ S(k) \} \Big/ \mathcal{F}\{ S(k) \} \big|_{\tau=0}
$$

For the Gaussian spectrum, the inverse Fourier transform yields:

$$
\gamma(\tau) = \exp\!\left[ - \left( \frac{\pi \Delta k \, \tau}{4 \sqrt{\ln 2}} \right)^{\!2} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Coherence length is the FWHM of $\vert \gamma(\tau) \vert$.**

Set $\vert \gamma(\tau_c) \vert = 1/2$:

$$
\exp\!\left[ - \left( \frac{\pi \Delta k \, \tau_c}{4 \sqrt{\ln 2}} \right)^{\!2} \right] = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Take the natural logarithm on both sides:

$$
- \left( \frac{\pi \Delta k \, \tau_c}{4 \sqrt{\ln 2}} \right)^{\!2} = \ln(1/2) = - \ln 2
$$

Drop the negative signs and solve for $\tau_c$:

$$
\left( \frac{\pi \Delta k \, \tau_c}{4 \sqrt{\ln 2}} \right)^{\!2} = \ln 2
$$

$$
\frac{\pi \Delta k \, \tau_c}{4 \sqrt{\ln 2}} = \sqrt{\ln 2}
$$

$$
\tau_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi \Delta k}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert coherence time to coherence length.**

The coherence length is the optical path length traversed in time $\tau_c$:

$$
l_c = c \, \tau_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{c}{\Delta k}
$$

Using $\Delta k = 2\pi \Delta \lambda / \lambda_0^{2}$ (from differentiating $k = 2\pi/\lambda$):

$$
l_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{c \, \lambda_0^{2}}{2 \pi \Delta \lambda} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{c \, \lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}
$$

A common and more compact form (using $c = f \lambda_0$, but simplified for OCT textbook usage) is:

$$
\boxed{\, l_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda} \,}
$$

**Step 5 — Axial resolution in OCT is half the coherence length (round-trip path-length difference).**

In a Michelson interferometer, the light traverses the path-length mismatch **twice** (once forward, once back). Therefore the depth resolution is:

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta z = \frac{l_c}{2} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda} \,}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (typical SLED at $\lambda_0 = 840$ nm, $\Delta \lambda = 50$ nm):**

$$
\Delta z = \frac{2 \times 0.693}{3.1416} \cdot \frac{(840 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{50 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 0.4413 \cdot \frac{7.056 \times 10^{-13}}{5.0 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 0.4413 \cdot 1.4112 \times 10^{-5} \approx 6.23 \, \mu m
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matches the experimentally observed 5–10 µm axial resolution of commercial ophthalmic OCT systems — a **validating cross-check** that the derivation is correct.

## 3.2 Derivation of the FD-OCT Signal and the A-scan

**Step 1 — The electric field returning from the reference arm.**

$$
E_r(k, t) = \frac{E_0(k)}{\sqrt{2}} \, r_r \, e^{i(2 k z_r - \omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 — The electric field returning from a single sample reflector at depth $z_s$.**

$$
E_s(k, t) = \frac{E_0(k)}{\sqrt{2}} \, r_s \, e^{i(2 k z_s - \omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 — The detected (intensity) signal is the time-averaged square of the sum.**

The photodetector is a square-law device; it records intensity:

$$
I_D(k) = \langle \vert E_r + E_s \vert^{2} \rangle_t
$$

Expanding:

$$
I_D(k) = \langle \vert E_r \vert^{2} \rangle + \langle \vert E_s \vert^{2} \rangle + 2 \Re \{ \langle E_r E_s^{*} \rangle \}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the field expressions and using $\Re\{e^{i\theta}\} = \cos\theta$:**

$$
I_D(k) = S(k) \left[ R_r + R_s + 2 \sqrt{R_r R_s} \cos(2 k \Delta z) \right]
$$

where $\Delta z = z_s - z_r$, and $R_i = \vert r_i \vert^{2}$.

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform to obtain the depth-reflectivity profile.**

The cosine-modulated term is the carrier of depth information. Performing an inverse Fourier transform with respect to $k$ (treating $z$ as the conjugate variable to $2k$):

$$
\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ I_D(k) \} = \text{DC terms} + \sqrt{R_r R_s} \, S(z) \otimes \left[ \delta(z - \Delta z) + \delta(z + \Delta z) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The transform yields **two mirror-image peaks**: one at $+\Delta z$ and one at $-\Delta z$. The negative-$z$ peak is the **complex conjugate artifact**, removed by:
> 1. Recording only real part of FFT (simple depth-equal path only).
> 2. Hilbert transform to extract the analytic signal.
> 3. Phase-shifting / heterodyne detection in SS-OCT.

**Step 6 — Log-compression converts the linear A-scan to a decibel-scaled image.**

$$
I_{dB}(z) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\vert \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{I_D(k)\} \vert}{I_{ref}} \right)
$$

This produces the grayscale intensity values seen on the B-scan display.

## 3.3 Derivation of the SNR Advantage of FD-OCT

**Step 1 — In TD-OCT, the signal at any instant comes from a single coherence volume, giving detected signal:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{TD} = \frac{\eta P_s \tau}{h \nu}
$$

where $\eta$ is detector quantum efficiency, $P_s$ is sample power, $\tau$ is the integration time per depth point, and $h\nu$ is the photon energy.

**Step 2 — In FD-OCT, the entire A-scan is recorded in one integration period $T$ using $N$ spectral samples.**

Because the same optical power is detected in parallel over $N$ spectral channels (and the Fourier transform is unitary), the effective signal is boosted:

$$
\text{SNR}_{FD} = N \cdot \frac{\eta P_s T}{h \nu}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of SNRs (assuming $T = \tau$ for a fair comparison):**

$$
\frac{\text{SNR}_{FD}}{\text{SNR}_{TD}} = N
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **decibels**, this advantage is $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. With $N = 1024$ spectral samples, $\text{SNR gain} \approx 30$ dB — a **1000× power advantage**, which is the cornerstone of why modern clinical OCT is dominated by FD-OCT architectures.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Maximum Imaging Depth (FD-OCT)

**Step 1 — The detected spectrum is sampled at finite resolution $\delta k$.**

The Nyquist criterion applied to the cosinusoidal fringe demands that the fringe frequency $f_{fringe} = \Delta z / \pi$ be sampled at least twice per cycle:

$$
\delta k \leq \frac{\pi}{z_{max}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solving for the maximum unambiguous depth:**

$$
\boxed{\, z_{max} = \frac{\pi}{\delta k} = \frac{\pi N}{2 \, \Delta k_{span}} \,}
$$

This is also the formula for the **sensitivity roll-off** $z_{1/2} \approx \pi / \delta k$ — the depth at which SNR drops to half its near-DC value due to finite spectrometer resolution (in SD-OCT) or finite instantaneous linewidth (in SS-OCT).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Common places where students lose marks in this topic:
> 1. **Confusing coherence length $l_c$ with axial resolution $\Delta z$.** Remember: $\Delta z = l_c/2$ in a Michelson-based OCT due to the round-trip path.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $k$-space.** The detected cosinusoid oscillates at frequency $2k\Delta z$ (not $k\Delta z$) because of the round trip — failure to include this shifts the recovered depth.
> 3. **Writing the SNR advantage as a generic "better" rather than a quantitative $10\log_{10}(N)$ dB.**
> 4. **Mixing up SD-OCT and SS-OCT architectures.** SD-OCT uses a broadband source + spectrometer; SS-OCT uses a swept narrowband laser + single detector. Markers will mark this distinction strictly.
> 5. **Skipping k-space resampling.** In SD-OCT, the spectrometer records intensity vs. $\lambda$ uniformly, but the FFT requires uniform $k$-spacing. Resampling is a mandatory pipeline step.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the complex conjugate artifact** when deriving the FD-OCT A-scan.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of Time-Domain OCT (TD-OCT). Mention its main limitation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TD-OCT uses a broadband low-coherence source (e.g., SLED) split by a 2×2 fiber coupler into a reference arm and a sample arm. The reference arm contains a **scanning reference mirror** mechanically translated over a range $Z_{scan}$. Light returning from both arms recombines at the coupler and is incident on a photodetector. Interference fringes are observed only when the optical path-length difference between the two arms is within the **coherence length** $l_c$ of the source — this provides depth gating. By translating the reference mirror, an **A-scan** (reflectivity vs. depth) is built point-by-point, and a B-scan is obtained by lateral scanning of the sample beam using a galvanometer. **Main limitation**: low SNR because light from only one depth contributes to the detected signal at any instant; mechanical scanning also limits acquisition speed (≤ a few kHz A-scan rate).

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"Differentiate between SD-OCT and SS-OCT in terms of architecture, light source, and detector used."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SD-OCT | SS-OCT |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Broadband SLED / supercontinuum | Narrowband, rapidly tunable swept laser |
| Detector | Spectrometer + line-scan CCD/CMOS | Single (balanced) photodetector + high-speed ADC |
| Wavelength selection | Spatial (grating + camera) | Temporal (laser tuning) |
| Sensitivity roll-off | Limited by spectrometer resolution $\delta\lambda$ | Limited by instantaneous laser linewidth |
| Imaging depth | 1–3 mm typical | 5–12 mm typical |
| Speed | Limited by camera line rate | Limited by laser sweep rate (often faster) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, describe the working of a **Spectral-Domain OCT (SD-OCT)** system. Explain the role of the spectrometer and the inverse Fourier transform in image formation. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for **axial resolution** in OCT in terms of central wavelength $\lambda_0$ and source bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$. A typical ophthalmic SLED source has $\lambda_0 = 840$ nm and $\Delta\lambda = 50$ nm. Compute the resulting axial resolution in tissue (assume refractive index $n = 1.36$). (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for B-A(a):**
1. A broadband, low-coherence source (SLED at $\sim$840 nm for ophthalmic use) feeds a fiber-based Michelson interferometer.
2. A 2×2 fiber coupler splits the light into a **reference arm** (fixed mirror) and a **sample arm** (galvanometer-scanned probe beam).
3. Back-scattered light from internal tissue layers recombines with reference-arm light.
4. The output is **not** sent to a single photodetector (as in TD-OCT) but to a **diffraction-grating-based spectrometer** that spatially disperses the interference spectrum $I_D(\lambda)$ across a **line-scan CCD/CMOS camera**.
5. The camera captures the **interference spectrum $I_D(k)$** in a single integration time (no axial scanning required).
6. The spectrum is **resampled from $\lambda$ to $k$** space (since the FFT requires linear $k$-spacing).
7. An **inverse FFT** is applied to extract the depth-resolved A-scan.
8. **Apodization** (e.g., Hann or Hamming window) suppresses side-lobes; **DC term** is removed; **complex conjugate** is eliminated.
9. The **log-compressed A-scan** is then stacked laterally to form a B-scan, and a 3-D volume is built by repeating the B-scan along the orthogonal lateral axis.

**Key roles:**
- **Spectrometer**: parallel acquisition of the interference spectrum, enabling simultaneous detection of all depths.
- **Inverse FFT**: converts the spectral-domain encoded depth information into the spatial-domain A-scan.

---

**Model Solution for B-A(b):**

The axial resolution in OCT is determined by the **coherence length** of the source.

For a source with Gaussian spectral profile, the coherence length is:

$$
l_c = \frac{4 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}
$$

Since the light in a Michelson interferometer undergoes a **round trip**, the axial resolution is half the coherence length:

$$
\Delta z_{\text{air}} = \frac{l_c}{2} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}
$$

Inside tissue, the optical path is divided by the refractive index $n$:

$$
\Delta z_{\text{tissue}} = \frac{\Delta z_{\text{air}}}{n} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi n} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}
$$

**Numerical substitution** (using $n = 1.36$ for retinal tissue):

$$
\Delta z_{\text{tissue}} = \frac{2 \times 0.693}{3.1416 \times 1.36} \cdot \frac{(840 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{50 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.386}{4.273} \cdot \frac{7.056 \times 10^{-13}}{5.0 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
= 0.3244 \cdot 1.4112 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta z_{\text{tissue}} \approx 4.58 \ \mu m \,}
$$

This is in excellent agreement with the $\sim$5 µm axial resolution quoted in commercial ophthalmic OCT specifications.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Alternative** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **FD-OCT signal equation** and describe how depth information is recovered by the inverse Fourier transform. Discuss the **complex conjugate artifact** and methods to remove it. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of **Swept-Source OCT (SS-OCT)**. Compare its sensitivity, roll-off, and imaging-depth performance with SD-OCT. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for B-B(a):**

The detected signal at the spectrometer/photodetector port of a fiber Michelson interferometer with a single sample reflector is:

$$
I_D(k) = S(k) \left[ R_r + R_s + 2 \sqrt{R_r R_s} \cos(2 k \Delta z) \right]
$$

The first two terms are **DC** components (non-interferometric). The third term is the **interferometric (AC) component**, which is a cosine function of wavenumber $k$ with frequency proportional to the path-length difference $\Delta z$.

**Depth recovery** is performed by taking the inverse Fourier transform with respect to $k$:

$$
\mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ I_D(k) \} \propto \sqrt{R_r R_s} \left[ \delta(z - \Delta z) + \delta(z + \Delta z) \right] + \text{DC}
$$

The transform yields two peaks: one at the true depth $+\Delta z$ and a **mirror (complex-conjugate) peak** at $-\Delta z$. These two peaks are mathematically indistinguishable in standard SD-OCT because the recorded $I_D(k)$ is a **real-valued** spectrum (Hermitian-symmetric), making its FFT intrinsically symmetric.

**Methods to remove the complex-conjugate artifact:**
1. **Phase-shifting (heterodyne) method**: Introduce a small linear phase ramp $\phi(k) = \alpha k$ in the reference arm using a rapid phase modulator. This shifts the positive-depth peak away from its conjugate, allowing separation.
2. **Hilbert transform**: Convert the real-valued spectrum into its **analytic signal** (complex-valued) by suppressing negative frequency components, leaving only the positive-depth peak.
3. **Off-pivot geometry / full-range complex method** (in SS-OCT using k-clock triggering).
4. **Back-scattered geometry** with a 50/50 non-reciprocal fiber setup.

**Model Solution for B-B(b):**

In SS-OCT, a **narrowband tunable laser** is the source. The laser's central wavelength is rapidly swept (sweep rates of 50–400 kHz) across a broad range (e.g., $\lambda = 1050$–1090 nm for retinal SS-OCT, or 1260–1360 nm for deep-tissue imaging). The swept light is split by a 2×2 coupler into reference and sample arms, recombines, and falls on a **single (typically balanced) photodetector**. The detected intensity as a function of time is re-mapped to $I(k)$ using a **k-clock trigger** (often from a separate fiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer). The A-scan is then obtained by inverse FFT, identical to SD-OCT downstream.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SD-OCT | SS-OCT |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Broadband SLED | Tunable swept laser |
| Detector | Spectrometer + line camera | Single photodetector + k-clock + ADC |
| Sensitivity roll-off | Faster (set by $\delta\lambda$) | Slower (set by laser instantaneous linewidth) |
| Maximum imaging depth | 1–3 mm | 5–12 mm |
| Sensitivity | 90–95 dB | 95–110 dB |
| A-scan rate | Up to $\sim$ 70 kHz (camera limit) | Up to $\sim$ MHz (laser sweep + high-speed ADC) |
| Cost | Lower (camera-based) | Higher (tunable laser + k-clock) |

**Conclusion:** SS-OCT is preferred when **deeper imaging** and **faster acquisition** are required, while SD-OCT remains a cost-effective choice for routine ophthalmic and dermatologic applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Image Generation in OCT"**

- **OCT is optical echo-location** (like ultrasound, but with light), gated by **coherence** instead of time.
- **Three architectures**: TD-OCT, SD-OCT, SS-OCT. **Modern systems are dominated by FD-OCT (SD + SS) due to the 20–30 dB SNR advantage.**
- **Axial resolution** $\Delta z = \dfrac{2 \ln 2}{\pi n} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}$. **Wider bandwidth = finer axial resolution.**
- **Lateral resolution** is set by the **objective NA** of the sample arm: $\Delta x = 4 \lambda_0 f / (\pi d)$.
- **Imaging depth** is limited by **scattering** in tissue (typically 1–3 mm) and by **spectral sampling resolution** (Nyquist on the fringe).
- **A-scan** = 1-D depth profile. **B-scan** = 2-D cross-section. **C-scan / en-face** = 2-D lateral slice. **Volume** = 3-D data cube.
- **FD-OCT signal equation**: $I_D(k) = S(k)[R_r + R_s + 2\sqrt{R_r R_s}\cos(2 k \Delta z)]$.
- **A-scan** = **inverse FFT** of $I_D(k)$ after k-space resampling and DC removal.
- **Complex conjugate artifact** arises from the real-valued nature of the spectrum; eliminated by phase shifting, Hilbert transform, or k-clock heterodyne detection.
- **SD-OCT** uses a spectrometer + line camera; **SS-OCT** uses a swept laser + single detector + k-clock.
- **SS-OCT** offers deeper imaging and faster acquisition, while **SD-OCT** is cheaper and adequate for most retinal applications.
- **Sensitivity roll-off** in FD-OCT: $z_{1/2} \approx \pi / \delta k$ — set by spectrometer pixel resolution (SD) or laser instantaneous linewidth (SS).
- **SNR advantage** of FD-OCT over TD-OCT: $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB where $N$ = number of spectral samples (≈ 30 dB for $N = 1024$).
- **Log-compression** (in dB) is the final display step, yielding the familiar grayscale B-scan.
- **Image formation pipeline**: source → interferometer → spectral detection → k-resample → inverse FFT → apodization → DC removal → conjugate removal → log → display.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OCT System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT524 - BIOMEDICAL OPTICS & BIOPHOTONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OCT System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)** is a non-invasive, non-contact, high-resolution biomedical imaging modality that performs cross-sectional (tomographic) imaging of biological tissues by measuring the **echo time delay** and **magnitude of back-scattered near-infrared (NIR) light** using the principles of **low-coherence (white-light) interferometry**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim concept):**
> OCT is a non-invasive imaging technique that uses low-coherence interferometry to produce high-resolution (~1–15 µm) cross-sectional images of internal micro-structures in biological tissue, with penetration depths of 1–3 mm.

### Key System Parameters (Standard KTU-Listed Metrics)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Axial Resolution ($\Delta z$) | **1 – 15** | µm |
| Lateral Resolution | **1 – 30** | µm |
| Penetration Depth (Tissue) | **1 – 3** | mm |
| Central Wavelength ($\lambda_0$) | **800 – 1300** (typically 840 or 1310) | nm |
| Source Bandwidth ($\Delta\lambda$) | **30 – 150** | nm |
| Acquisition Speed (A-scan) | **20 – 400,000** | scans/s |
| Sensitivity | **90 – 110** | dB |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"OCT is to Optical Biopsy as Ultrasound is to Sonography"**

Imagine a **fish-finder sonar** on a boat. The sonar sends a sound "ping" downward, and the time it takes for the echo to return tells you how deep the fish is, and how strong the echo is tells you how big/dense the fish is. The boat then moves a little, pings again, and stacks all the lines into a 2-D picture of the lake bed.

**OCT does exactly the same thing — but instead of using sound waves, it uses LIGHT waves.**

- Instead of a sound "ping", OCT uses a pulse of **low-coherence (broadband) infrared light**.
- Instead of timing sound echoes (microseconds), OCT times **light echoes** using an *optical trick* called **interferometry** (because light is too fast — 3×10⁸ m/s — for any electronic clock).
- The "boat" is a **scanning mirror** in the sample arm that sweeps a light beam across the tissue.
- The "lake bed picture" is the **cross-sectional image of the retina / skin / coronary artery**.

The reason **infrared** is used: it penetrates tissue deeper (water and hemoglobin absorb less NIR) and is **eye-safe** at the powers used (typically < 1 mW, well below the **ANSI MPE limit of 5 mW** for 840 nm).

### Why "Low-Coherence"?

If you used a laser (high-coherence, narrow-band), the interference pattern would be huge (meters long) and you couldn't pinpoint depth. By using a **broadband source** (e.g., a Superluminescent Diode, SLD), the interference signal exists **only over a very short distance** — the **coherence length** ($l_c$) — which sets the *axial resolution*. This is the **fundamental trick** of OCT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

OCT is built on the **Michelson Interferometer** configuration. The optical path is broken into the following structured sequence:

1. **Broadband Source Emission**: A Superluminescent Diode (SLD) or swept-source laser emits low-coherence light centered at wavelength $\lambda_0$ with bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$ (FWHM). This is split 50:50 by a fiber coupler (acting as a beam-splitter).
2. **Reference Arm**: 50% of the light travels to a **reference mirror** mounted on a scanning mechanism. The mirror is at a known optical path length $L_R$.
3. **Sample Arm**: The other 50% is focused by an objective lens into the **biological tissue** at optical path length $L_S(z)$, where $z$ is the depth coordinate.
4. **Back-Scatter Collection**: Light back-scattered from the tissue microstructure (at depth $z$) and light reflected from the reference mirror recombine at the fiber coupler.
5. **Interference Detection**: The recombined light falls on a photodetector. **Constructive interference occurs ONLY when** $\vert L_S - L_R \vert \le l_c / 2$ — i.e., when the path difference is within the **coherence length**.
6. **Depth Discrimination (A-scan)**: By translating the reference mirror (Time-Domain OCT) or by spectral analysis (Fourier-Domain OCT), the system scans a 1-D reflectivity profile $R(z)$ along depth — this is one **A-scan**.
7. **Lateral Scanning (B-scan)**: A galvanometer mirror scans the sample beam across the tissue surface, stacking A-scans into a 2-D **cross-sectional image (B-scan)**.
8. **3-D Volume**: A second galvo scans the orthogonal direction to form a 3-D volumetric dataset (**C-scan** or volume).

## 2.2 The Three Architectural Families of OCT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 must-know: Distinguish TD-OCT, SD-OCT, and SS-OCT — each is a guaranteed exam question.**

### A. Time-Domain OCT (TD-OCT)
- Reference mirror is **physically translated** axially.
- Depth is encoded in the **time-delay** of interference fringes.
- Speed limited to **~400 A-scans/s**.
- **Disadvantage**: Moving parts → mechanical wear; low speed.

### B. Spectral-Domain OCT (SD-OCT)
- Reference mirror is **stationary**.
- A **spectrometer** (grating + line-scan CCD) measures the interferogram $I(\lambda)$ vs wavelength.
- Depth information is retrieved by **Fourier Transform** of the spectral interferogram.
- Speed: **20,000 – 100,000 A-scans/s**.
- **Advantage**: Massive SNR advantage (called *sensitivity advantage*, ~20–30 dB over TD-OCT).

### C. Swept-Source OCT (SS-OCT)
- Uses a **rapidly tunable narrowband laser** that sweeps $\lambda$ over time.
- Detector is a single photodiode (fast).
- Speed: **100,000 – 400,000+ A-scans/s**.
- **Advantage**: Deeper penetration (1300 nm regime), no sensitivity roll-off at edges of spectrometer range.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 board-exam standard**. Memorize the boxed expressions; derive the rest.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coherence Length ($l_c$) | $l_c = \dfrac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}$ | m | FWHM Gaussian source |
| 2 | Axial Resolution ($\Delta z$) | $\Delta z = \dfrac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda} = \dfrac{l_c}{2}$ | m | Half the round-trip coherence length |
| 3 | Total A-scan Range (SD-OCT) | $z_{max} = \dfrac{\lambda_0^2}{4 \, \delta\lambda}$ | m | $\delta\lambda$ = spectrometer res. |
| 4 | Lateral Resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{4 \lambda_0}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{f}{d}$ | m | $f$ = lens focal length, $d$ = beam dia. |
| 5 | Depth of Focus (Rayleigh) | $b = \dfrac{\pi \Delta x^2}{2 \lambda_0}$ | m | Confocal parameter |
| 6 | OCT Sensitivity (SNR) | $SNR = \dfrac{\eta P_s}{2 h \nu \, B}$ | dB | $\eta$ = quantum eff., $B$ = bandwidth |
| 7 | Sensitivity Fall-off (SD-OCT) | $SNR(z) \propto \left[\dfrac{\sin(\pi z / z_{max})}{\pi z / z_{max}}\right]^2$ | — | sinc² decay |
| 8 | Number of Pixels (A-scan) | $N = \dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{\delta\lambda}$ | — | SD-OCT pixel count |
| 9 | Maximum A-scan Rate (SS-OCT) | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{v_{sweep}}{\Delta\lambda}$ | Hz | $v_{sweep}$ = sweep speed |
| 10 | Optical Power Limit (Eye-safe) | $P < 5$ mW (840 nm) | mW | ANSI MPE for retinal exposure |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ophthalmology**: Diagnosing macular degeneration, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy.
- **Cardiology**: Intravascular OCT (IV-OCT) for plaque characterization in coronary arteries.
- **Dermatology**: Skin cancer (melanoma) depth profiling.
- **Dentistry & Endoscopy**: Subsurface imaging of soft tissue and mucosa.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Axial Resolution from Coherence Length

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-asked derivation** in the KTU ESE for OCT. Memorize the step ordering.

### Starting Point — The Mutually Coherent Condition

Two optical waves reflected from the reference arm and from a depth $z$ inside the tissue will produce **observable interference** at the detector **if and only if** their optical path difference $\Delta L = 2 z$ is smaller than the **coherence length** $l_c$ of the source. The factor of 2 arises because light travels *down and back* through the sample.

The condition is therefore written as:

$$
\Delta L = 2 z \le l_c
$$

Hence the **maximum resolvable round-trip depth** is one coherence length, but the **resolution itself** (the FWHM of the interferometric envelope) corresponds to half of this because the reference mirror must be moved across half the envelope to recover the peak.

### Step 1 — Define the Coherence Length for a Gaussian Source

A broadband OCT source (SLD or Ti:Sapphire Kerr-lens mode-locked laser) has a **Gaussian spectral profile**. The spectral power density is:

$$
S(\lambda) = S_0 \exp\!\left[-4 \ln 2 \left(\frac{\lambda - \lambda_0}{\Delta\lambda}\right)^{\!2}\right]
$$

where $\Delta\lambda$ is the **FWHM spectral bandwidth** of the source.

### Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform to Get the Coherence Function

The complex degree of temporal coherence $g(\tau)$ is the Fourier transform of the normalized spectral density. For a Gaussian spectrum, $g(\tau)$ is also a Gaussian in optical delay $\tau$:

$$
g(\tau) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{\pi \Delta\nu \tau}{2 \sqrt{\ln 2}}\right)^{\!2}\right]
$$

where $\Delta\nu$ is the **FWHM spectral bandwidth in frequency** related to $\Delta\lambda$ by:

$$
\Delta\nu = \frac{c \, \Delta\lambda}{\lambda_0^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the FWHM of $g(\tau)$ in Terms of Optical Path

Setting $g(\tau_{coh}) = 1/2$ and solving:

$$
\left(\frac{\pi \Delta\nu \tau_{coh}}{2 \sqrt{\ln 2}}\right)^{\!2} = \ln 2
$$

$$
\tau_{coh} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi \Delta\nu} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{c \, \Delta\lambda}
$$

Multiplying by the speed of light $c$ converts time delay to one-way optical path. The **round-trip coherence length** is therefore:

$$
l_c = c \cdot \tau_{coh} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Round-Trip Coherence Length to Axial Resolution

In a Michelson interferometer, light makes a round trip. Hence the **depth that produces interference** is half the round-trip coherence length:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta z = \frac{l_c}{2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}\;}
$$

Numerical sanity check (typical retinal OCT):
- $\lambda_0 = 840$ nm
- $\Delta\lambda = 50$ nm
- $\Delta z = (0.221) \times (840^2 / 50)$ nm
- $\Delta z \approx 3.1$ µm ✓ (matches commercial systems)

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Imaging Depth in Spectral-Domain OCT

In SD-OCT, the spectrometer samples the spectrum at discrete wavelength pixels with resolution $\delta\lambda$. The maximum unambiguous depth is set by the **Nyquist sampling criterion** on the spectral interferogram, which oscillates at a frequency proportional to the path mismatch $\Delta z$:

$$
\Delta z_{max} = \frac{\lambda_0^2}{4 \, \delta\lambda}
$$

**Derivation sketch:**

The argument of the spectral interferogram phase contains $\exp(j \, 2 k \, \Delta z) = \exp\!\big(j \, 4\pi \Delta z / \lambda\big)$. Sampling in wavelength $\delta\lambda$ translates to a wavelength-domain sampling interval. Nyquist demands two samples per oscillation in the inverse-Fourier domain; solving gives the boxed result above.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Three OCT Architectures

> [!NOTE]
> This table is **directly examinable** as a 7-mark question. Replicate the comparison style in your answer.

| Parameter | TD-OCT | SD-OCT | SS-OCT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference mirror | Moving | Stationary | Stationary |
| Depth encoding | Time delay | Fourier of $I(\lambda)$ | Fourier of $I(t)$ |
| Detector | Single photodiode | Line-scan CCD | Single fast photodiode |
| Speed (A-scans/s) | 400 – 4,000 | 20,000 – 70,000 | 100,000 – 400,000+ |
| Sensitivity (dB) | 90 – 95 | 100 – 110 | 100 – 110 |
| Sensitivity roll-off | None | sinc² (worse at depth) | sinc² (milder) |
| Typical $\lambda_0$ | 800 – 1300 nm | 800 – 870 nm | 1000 – 1310 nm |
| Best use | Early research | Ophthalmology (retina) | Deep tissue, IV-OCT |
| Moving parts | Yes (reference) | No | Tunable laser only |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-mark problem)

> **Problem:** An OCT system uses a Superluminescent Diode with central wavelength $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm and FWHM bandwidth $\Delta\lambda = 70$ nm. Compute (a) the axial resolution, and (b) the maximum imaging depth if the spectrometer has a wavelength resolution $\delta\lambda = 0.1$ nm.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Axial resolution.

$$
\Delta z = \frac{\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}
$$

$$
\Delta z = \frac{0.2206}{1} \cdot \frac{(1310 \times 10^{-9})^2}{70 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 0.2206 \times \frac{1.7161 \times 10^{-12}}{70 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\Delta z = 0.2206 \times 2.4516 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta z \approx 5.41 \; \mu\text{m}\;}
$$

**Part (b):** Maximum imaging depth (Nyquist).

$$
z_{max} = \frac{\lambda_0^2}{4 \, \delta\lambda}
$$

$$
z_{max} = \frac{(1310 \times 10^{-9})^2}{4 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
z_{max} = \frac{1.7161 \times 10^{-12}}{4 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;z_{max} \approx 4.29 \; \text{mm}\;}
$$

This combination (1310 nm, 70 nm) is typical for **intravascular OCT (IV-OCT)** because the longer wavelength penetrates deeper into the coronary artery wall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic OCT System

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the principal signal-flow interactions of a generic OCT engine. (The KTU board often accepts such block diagrams in lieu of full physical schematic drawings.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Superluminescent Diode SLD Source] -->|Broadband NIR Light| coupA[2x2 Fiber Coupler 50:50 Splitter]
    coupA -->|Reference Path| refA[Reference Arm Mirror on Translation Stage]
    coupA -->|Sample Path| sampA[Sample Arm Collimator and Galvo Scanner]
    sampA -->|Focused Beam| tissueA[Biological Tissue with Depth Axis z]
    tissueA -->|Back-Scattered Field| sampA
    refA -->|Recombined Field| coupA
    coupA -->|Interferogram Signal| detA[Photodetector or Spectrometer]
    detA -->|Digitized Signal| dspA[Signal Processing Unit]
    dspA -->|Fourier Transform for FD OCT| imgA[2D B-scan Cross Sectional Image]
    dspA -->|Volume Render| volA[3D Volumetric OCT Dataset]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flow — Three OCT Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[OCT System Architectures] --> tdA[Time Domain OCT TD OCT]
    rootA --> sdA[Spectral Domain OCT SD OCT]
    rootA --> ssA[Swept Source OCT SS OCT]
    tdA --> tdF1[Reference mirror translated axially]
    tdA --> tdF2[Depth encoded in time delay]
    tdA --> tdF3[Speed: 400 to 4000 A-scans per second]
    tdA --> tdF4[Single photodiode detector]
    sdA --> sdF1[Stationary reference mirror]
    sdA --> sdF2[Spectrometer with line scan CCD]
    sdA --> sdF3[Depth by Fourier transform of spectrum]
    sdA --> sdF4[Speed: 20000 to 70000 A-scans per second]
    ssA --> ssF1[Swept tunable narrowband laser]
    ssA --> ssF2[Single fast photodiode detector]
    ssA --> ssF3[Depth by Fourier transform in time]
    ssA --> ssF4[Speed: 100000 to 400000 A-scans per second]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spA[Raw Spectral Interferogram I of lambda] --> spB[Subtract DC Background]
    spB --> spC[Resample to Uniform k-space Wavenumber]
    spC --> spD[Multiply by Hanning Window Function]
    spD --> spE[Discrete Fourier Transform FFT]
    spE --> spF[Extract Modulus Magnitude A-scan Profile]
    spF --> spG[Logarithmic Compression dB Scale]
    spG --> spH[Stack A-scans into 2D B-scan Image]
```

## 4.4 Light Path Mapping in the Sample Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fibA[Single Mode Fiber] --> colA[Collimating Lens]
    colA --> galA[X-Scanning Galvo Mirror]
    galA --> galB[Y-Scanning Galvo Mirror]
    galB --> objA[Scanning Objective Lens]
    objA --> focA[Focal Spot on Tissue at Depth z]
    focA --> backA[Back-Scattered Light Returns to Objective]
    backA --> fibA
```

**Reading the diagrams (KTU-style caption):**
- The **light blue arrows** show the forward (delivery) path of the probing beam.
- The **red arrows** show the back-scattered return path that carries the depth-encoded sample information.
- The **detector** in a TD-OCT is a single photodiode; in SD-OCT it is replaced by the spectrometer; in SS-OCT it is again a single photodiode but preceded by a tunable laser.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024 model]**

### Q1. Define Optical Coherence Tomography. Mention any two key clinical applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) is a non-invasive, non-contact biomedical imaging technique that uses the principle of **low-coherence interferometry** to produce **high-resolution cross-sectional images** of internal microstructure of biological tissues. It is often described as the "optical analogue of ultrasound," but it uses **near-infrared light** instead of sound waves, achieving axial resolutions of **1–15 µm** and penetration depths of **1–3 mm** in scattering tissue.

**Two clinical applications (1 mark):**
1. **Ophthalmology** — diagnosis and monitoring of retinal diseases such as macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, and glaucoma (RNFL thickness measurement).
2. **Cardiology** — *Intravascular OCT (IV-OCT)* for characterizing atherosclerotic plaque morphology inside coronary arteries, including identification of thin-cap fibroatheroma and stent apposition.

*(Alternative: Dermatology for melanoma depth profiling; Dentistry for enamel/dentin imaging.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

### Q2. What is the role of a Superluminescent Diode (SLD) in an OCT system? Why is a narrow-linewidth laser unsuitable?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of SLD (2 marks):**
A Superluminescent Diode (SLD) acts as the **broadband low-coherence optical source** in an OCT system. It emits **near-infrared light** with a broad spectral bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$ (typically 30–100 nm) centered at $\lambda_0$. The broad bandwidth is what produces a **short coherence length**, which directly determines the **axial resolution** of the system via $\Delta z = (\ln 2 / \pi) \cdot (\lambda_0^2 / \Delta\lambda)$. A broader $\Delta\lambda$ gives finer axial resolution.

**Why narrow-linewidth laser is unsuitable (1 mark):**
A narrow-linewidth laser has $\Delta\lambda \to 0$, so the coherence length $l_c \to \infty$ (meters to kilometers). This means interference fringes exist over a huge range of path differences, destroying the **depth discrimination** ability of the interferometer. The OCT system would lose all axial resolution and could not localize reflections to micrometer-scale depths in tissue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B is a **Module Internal Choice**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – April 2024 model]*

#### (a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Spectral-Domain OCT (SD-OCT) system. Compare its performance with Time-Domain OCT (TD-OCT).

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw using the architecture from Section 4.1, but with a spectrometer in place of a moving mirror + single photodiode.)*

```
SLD → 2×2 Coupler → (i) Reference arm (stationary mirror)
                   → (ii) Sample arm (galvo scanner + tissue)
        ← Recombined light → Spectrometer (grating + line CCD)
        → PC with FFT processing → B-scan image
```

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Light from the broadband SLD source is split by a 2×2 fiber coupler into a **reference arm** (with a *stationary* mirror) and a **sample arm** (with a galvo scanner).
2. The sample arm beam is focused into the tissue; back-scattered light from all depths recombines with the reference beam at the coupler.
3. The recombined beam is **spectrally dispersed** by a grating onto a line-scan CCD, recording the **spectral interferogram** $I(\lambda)$.
4. The interferogram is resampled from $\lambda$-space to uniform **wavenumber $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ space** to avoid depth-axis distortion.
5. A **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** of the resampled $I(k)$ yields the **depth-resolved reflectivity profile** (A-scan).
6. Repeating A-scans as the galvo sweeps the beam laterally gives the **2-D cross-sectional B-scan image**.

**Comparison with TD-OCT (2 marks):**

| Feature | TD-OCT | SD-OCT |
|---|---|---|
| Reference mirror | Moving | Stationary |
| Speed | 400–4,000 A/s | 20,000–70,000 A/s |
| Sensitivity | 90–95 dB | 100–110 dB (~25 dB higher) |
| SNR mechanism | Single-element detection | Multiplex (Fellgett) + Throughput (Jacquinot) advantages |
| Mechanical stability | Poor (moving parts) | Good (no moving parts) |

**Conclusion (extra credit):** SD-OCT is preferred in modern ophthalmic systems due to its superior speed and sensitivity.

#### (b) [7 marks] An OCT system uses a Ti:Sapphire-like source with $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm and $\Delta\lambda_{FWHM} = 100$ nm. Calculate (i) the coherence length, (ii) the axial resolution in tissue (assume refractive index $n = 1.36$), and (iii) comment on the suitability for retinal imaging.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Coherence length (2 marks):**

$$
l_c = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}
$$

$$
l_c = 0.4413 \cdot \frac{(800 \times 10^{-9})^2}{100 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
l_c = 0.4413 \cdot \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-7}}
$$

$$
l_c = 0.4413 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-6} = 2.82 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;l_c \approx 2.82 \; \mu\text{m}\;}
$$

**Part (ii) — Axial resolution in tissue (3 marks):**

In free space, the axial resolution is:

$$
\Delta z_{air} = \frac{\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda} = \frac{l_c}{2} = 1.41 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

In tissue, the optical path length is shortened by the refractive index $n$. The geometric (true physical) axial resolution becomes:

$$
\Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{\Delta z_{air}}{n}
$$

$$
\Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{1.41 \; \mu\text{m}}{1.36}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta z_{tissue} \approx 1.04 \; \mu\text{m}\;}
$$

**Part (iii) — Suitability for retinal imaging (2 marks):**

This system is **highly suitable** for retinal imaging because:
1. $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm lies in the **"optical window"** of the retina (600–1100 nm), where water and hemoglobin absorption are minimal and scattering is moderate — permitting imaging through clear ocular media to the retina.
2. The sub-cellular axial resolution (~1 µm in tissue, ~1.4 µm in air) is sufficient to resolve individual **retinal layers** (e.g., the ganglion cell layer, inner/outer photoreceptor segments) whose thicknesses range from 10–50 µm.
3. The source power can be kept below the **ANSI MPE limit (5 mW at 840 nm)** for safe retinal exposure.
4. A slight improvement could be made by using a **broader bandwidth** ($\Delta\lambda \approx 150$ nm, e.g., a Kerr-lens mode-locked Ti:Sapphire) to push resolution below 1 µm — used in *ultrahigh-resolution OCT (UHR-OCT)* research systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]*

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the principle of low-coherence interferometry used in OCT. Derive an expression for the axial resolution in terms of source central wavelength and bandwidth.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle of low-coherence interferometry (4 marks):**

Low-coherence interferometry exploits the **temporal coherence properties of broadband light** to perform precise measurement of optical path length. The principle is as follows:

1. A **broadband optical source** (SLD, Ti:Sapphire laser, or LED) with central wavelength $\lambda_0$ and FWHM bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$ emits light with a **short coherence length** $l_c \sim \lambda_0^2 / \Delta\lambda$.
2. The light is split into a **reference arm** and a **sample arm** using a Michelson interferometer configuration.
3. Light returning from the reference mirror (path $L_R$) and from a scatterer at depth $z$ inside the sample (path $L_S = 2z$ round-trip) is recombined.
4. The photodetector registers an **interferometric signal** — modulated by a carrier fringe pattern enveloped by the **coherence function** $g(\Delta L)$ — **only when** the path mismatch $\vert L_S - L_R \vert$ is **less than the coherence length** $l_c$.
5. Outside this region, the random phases from different spectral components average out and **no interference fringes are observed** — this provides the *optical gating* that enables depth discrimination.
6. By scanning the reference mirror (TD-OCT) or by Fourier-transforming the spectral interferogram (FD-OCT), a **reflectivity-vs-depth profile** $R(z)$ is reconstructed — this is the **A-scan**.

**Derivation of axial resolution (3 marks):** *(Write the full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta z = \frac{\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}\;}
$$

where $\lambda_0$ is the central wavelength and $\Delta\lambda$ is the FWHM bandwidth of the source. A larger bandwidth yields finer (smaller) axial resolution.

#### (b) [7 marks] Compare Time-Domain, Spectral-Domain, and Swept-Source OCT systems with respect to (i) imaging speed, (ii) sensitivity, and (iii) mechanism of depth encoding. State **two advantages** and **one disadvantage** of each architecture.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | TD-OCT | SD-OCT | SS-OCT |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Imaging speed | 400 – 4,000 A/s | 20,000 – 70,000 A/s | 100,000 – 400,000+ A/s |
| (ii) Sensitivity | 90 – 95 dB | 100 – 110 dB | 100 – 110 dB |
| (iii) Depth encoding | Time delay of fringe (moving reference mirror) | Fourier transform of $I(\lambda)$ (spectrometer) | Fourier transform of $I(t)$ (swept laser) |

**Advantages / Disadvantages (3 marks):**

**TD-OCT:**
- ✅ *Advantages:* Simple to implement; no sensitivity roll-off with depth; well-understood theory.
- ❌ *Disadvantage:* Slow speed (limited by mechanical translation of reference arm); sensitive to mechanical vibrations.

**SD-OCT:**
- ✅ *Advantages:* No moving parts (mechanically robust); 20–30 dB sensitivity advantage over TD-OCT; widely adopted in clinical ophthalmology.
- ❌ *Disadvantage:* Sensitivity *roll-off* with depth (sinc² envelope); limited by spectrometer line-array size.

**SS-OCT:**
- ✅ *Advantages:* Fastest acquisition (>100 kHz A-scan rate); uses longer wavelengths (1310 nm) → deeper tissue penetration; milder sensitivity roll-off than SD-OCT.
- ❌ *Disadvantage:* Swept-source lasers are expensive and require complex drive electronics (e.g., MEMS-tunable, polygon, or Fourier-domain mode-locked lasers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Important for marks):**
>
> - Students **commonly lose 1–2 marks** by writing "sensitivity roll-off in SD-OCT is zero" — this is **wrong**. SD-OCT has sinc² roll-off; SS-OCT also has roll-off but milder due to longer coherence of the swept source.
> - Students often confuse **sensitivity** (in dB) with **speed** (A-scans/s). Sensitivity is a *signal-to-noise metric*; speed is a *temporal acquisition rate*. Do not interchange them.
> - In the derivation of axial resolution, do **not** forget the **factor of 2** between round-trip coherence length and axial resolution ($\Delta z = l_c / 2$). A missing factor of 2 is the #1 deduction in this module.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read 3 times before the exam.**

- ☐ **OCT = "Optical Ultrasound"** — uses light echoes instead of sound echoes; depth from low-coherence interferometry.
- ☐ **Core Equation (board-favorite):** $\Delta z = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta\lambda}$ — broader bandwidth gives finer resolution.
- ☐ **Three architectures:** TD-OCT (slow, moving mirror), SD-OCT (spectrometer + FFT), SS-OCT (swept laser + FFT).
- ☐ **SD-OCT has 20–30 dB sensitivity advantage** over TD-OCT — called the "sensitivity advantage" from Fellgett + Jacquinot.
- ☐ **Wavelengths used:** 800–870 nm (retinal OCT, ophthalmology); 1310 nm (deep tissue, IV-OCT).
- ☐ **Penetration depth:** 1–3 mm in scattering tissue; 1300 nm penetrates deeper than 800 nm.
- ☐ **Lateral resolution** is set by the **focused spot size** $\Delta x = (4 \lambda_0 f) / (\pi d)$; not by coherence.
- ☐ **Eye-safety limit:** < 5 mW at 840 nm (ANSI MPE).
- ☐ **Nyquist imaging depth in SD-OCT:** $z_{max} = \lambda_0^2 / (4 \delta\lambda)$.
- ☐ **Sensitivity fall-off (SD-OCT):** $SNR(z) \propto \left[\sin(\pi z / z_{max}) / (\pi z / z_{max})\right]^2$ — sinc² envelope.
- ☐ **Number of A-scan pixels** in SD-OCT = $\Delta\lambda / \delta\lambda$.
- ☐ **Fourier-domain OCT** = generic term for SD-OCT + SS-OCT; *not* a separate architecture.
- ☐ **Key clinical applications:** Ophthalmology (retinal layers), IV-OCT (coronary plaque), dermatology (melanoma depth), endoscopy.
- ☐ **Refractive index correction:** $\Delta z_{tissue} = \Delta z_{air} / n$; for retina use $n \approx 1.36$.
- ☐ **SLD vs Laser:** SLD has broad bandwidth (good for OCT axial resolution); narrow-linewidth laser is unsuitable (coherence length too long).
- ☐ **Volumetric imaging:** 1-D A-scan (depth) + 1-D B-scan (lateral cross-section) + C-scan (volume) = 3-D OCT dataset.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrahigh-Resolution Optical Coherence Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT524 - BIOMEDICAL OPTICS & BIOPHOTONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrahigh-Resolution Optical Coherence Tomography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrahigh-Resolution Optical Coherence Tomography (UHR-OCT)** is a specialized class of Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) that achieves **sub-cellular axial resolution** — typically **1–3 μm in tissue** — by employing ultra-broadband, low-coherence light sources whose spectral bandwidths span **100–500 nm** full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM). It extends the conventional OCT axial resolution limit of ~10 μm by an order of magnitude, enabling visualization of individual cellular and sub-cellular structures *non-invasively* and *in vivo*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *UHR-OCT is a coherence-domain imaging modality in which the axial point-spread-function is governed by the temporal coherence envelope of an ultra-broadband source, allowing micrometer-scale, depth-resolved cross-sectional imaging of biological tissue based on backscattered near-infrared light.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering-Gallery Effect"

Imagine you are standing at the center of a giant circular room, shouting a sharp, broad-band noise burst. An echo comes back from the walls. **Standard OCT** uses a relatively "pure tone" (narrow band) — echoes from walls at different distances smear together, so you can only tell walls apart to within ~10 m. **UHR-OCT** uses a "broad static hiss" (super-broadband, like white noise): because the burst is so short in time, the echoes don't overlap unless the walls are extremely close. You can now distinguish walls that are only **1–2 micrometers apart** — the scale of a single cell nucleus.

The "broadness" of the static = **spectral bandwidth (Δλ)**. The "time duration" of the noise burst = **coherence length (Lc)**. They are inversely related: **broader spectrum ⇒ shorter coherence length ⇒ finer axial resolution**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

- **Central wavelength (λ₀):** Typically **800 nm** (Ti:Sapphire regime) for retinal/UHR, or **1300 nm** for deeper-tissue UHR.
- **Spectral bandwidth (Δλ):** **100–500 nm** (FWHM), compared to ~30 nm in standard OCT.
- **Coherence length (Lc):** Shrinks to **1–3 μm** in tissue (factor ~1/n of in-vacuum value).
- **Coherence function:** Gaussian envelope for Ti:Sapphire; quasi-Gaussian for SLD.
- **Axial resolution in tissue:** **Δz ≈ 1–3 μm**.
- **Lateral resolution:** Limited by optics (NA), independent of bandwidth — typically **3–10 μm** in UHR systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Ultra"-High?** The threshold is set by **biological relevance**: a human cell is ~10 μm in diameter. Standard OCT sees the cell as a single blob; UHR-OCT can resolve its **nucleus, organelles, and membranes** — a leap in diagnostic content.

### 1.4 Historical Milestones (Conceptual)

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| 1991 | First OCT (Huang et al.) | ~30 μm resolution using SLD |
| 1997 | **First UHR-OCT** (Drexler et al.) | Used femtosecond Ti:Sapphire, **1–2 μm** in vitro |
| 1999 | In-vivo retinal UHR-OCT | Visualized **retinal layers & photoreceptors** |
| 2001 | 1300 nm UHR-OCT for non-ocular | Enabled deeper-tissue UHR |
| 2003+ | Supercontinuum-based UHR | **>500 nm bandwidth**, sub-μm resolution |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Coherence-Gating Engine

OCT is fundamentally a **low-coherence interferometry** technique. The interference signal between the sample arm and reference arm exists **only when the optical path length difference is less than the coherence length** of the source. UHR-OCT pushes this gating window down to the **micrometer scale**.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. **Broadband light** from a super-broadband source (Ti:Sapphire, SLD, supercontinuum) is split into a **reference arm** and a **sample arm** by a fiber coupler (Michelson interferometer).
2. Light backscattered from a single scattering plane inside the tissue returns to the coupler with a delay **τ = 2z/c** (where z is depth).
3. Light reflected from the reference mirror returns with delay **τ_ref = 2z_ref/c**.
4. **Interference** occurs only when **|τ − τ_ref| < τ_c**, where τ_c is the **coherence time** of the source.
5. By scanning the reference mirror (time-domain UHR-OCT) or Fourier-transforming the spectral interferogram (spectral-domain UHR-OCT), one reconstructs the **reflectivity vs. depth profile** A(z).
6. The **full-width at half-maximum** of the point-spread function A(z) defines the **axial resolution Δz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The central insight:** *Axial resolution in OCT is decoupled from numerical aperture and lateral optics — it is set entirely by the temporal coherence properties of the source.* This is the conceptual heart of UHR-OCT.

### 2.2 Mathematical Origin of UHR Resolution

The detected photocurrent in an OCT interferometer is:

$$I_D(\Delta z) = I_R + I_S + 2\sqrt{I_R I_S} \, \text{Re}\{\gamma(\Delta z)\} \cdot \cos(k_0 \cdot 2\Delta z)$$

where:
- $I_R$ = reference intensity, $I_S$ = sample intensity
- $\gamma(\Delta z)$ = **complex degree of coherence** (the envelope)
- $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda_0$ = central wavenumber
- $\Delta z$ = path-length mismatch

The **envelope** $|\gamma(\Delta z)|$ is the Fourier transform of the **normalized source power spectrum** $S(\lambda)$:

$$\gamma(\Delta z) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \, e^{-j 2\pi \nu \Delta z / c} \, d\nu \quad \text{— (Fourier pair with the spectrum)}$$

For a **Gaussian source spectrum** of central frequency $\nu_0$ and FWHM spectral width $\Delta\nu$:

$$\vert \gamma(\Delta z) \vert = \exp\left[-\pi \left(\frac{\Delta \nu \, \Delta z}{2\sqrt{\ln 2} \, c}\right)^{2}\right]$$

The **FWHM of the envelope** (i.e., the **coherence length** $L_c$) is:

$$L_c = \frac{2\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{c}{\Delta \nu} = \frac{2\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}$$

> This is **identical** to the axial resolution Δz. In a medium of refractive index n, the in-tissue resolution becomes $\Delta z_{tissue} = L_c / n$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbols & Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coherence length (in vacuum) | $L_c = \dfrac{2\ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda} \approx 0.44 \, \dfrac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}$ | $\lambda_0$ [m], $\Delta\lambda$ [m] (FWHM) | **Same as axial resolution** in free space |
| Axial resolution in tissue | $\Delta z = \dfrac{L_c}{n} = \dfrac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^{2}}{n \, \Delta \lambda}$ | $n$ = refractive index (~1.35–1.4 for tissue) | **Key UHR formula** |
| Lateral resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{4\lambda}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{f}{D} = 1.22 \dfrac{\lambda_0}{\text{NA}}$ | $f$ = focal length, $D$ = beam dia, NA = numerical aperture | Set by optics, not bandwidth |
| Coherence time | $\tau_c = L_c / c$ | [s] | Inverse of spectral width |
| Sensitivity (shot-noise limited) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{\eta P_0}{h \nu \, \text{NEB}}$ | $\eta$ = quantum eff., $P_0$ = power, NEB = noise-equivalent bandwidth | Governs dynamic range |
| Maximum imaging depth (TD-OCT) | $z_{max} = \dfrac{l_{coh}}{2} = \dfrac{L_c}{2}$ | Limited by source bandwidth | Trade-off with resolution |
| SD-OCT axial pixel count | $N_{z} = \dfrac{\Delta \lambda}{\delta \lambda_{res}}$ | $\delta\lambda_{res}$ = spectrometer resolution | Higher bandwidth ⇒ more pixels |
| GDD-induced resolution penalty | $\Delta z_{GDD} = \sqrt{(\Delta z_{source})^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta \phi'' \, \Delta \omega^{2}}{2}\right)^{2}}$ | $\Delta\phi''$ = group-delay dispersion [s²] | Must be **compensated** |
| Single-pass tissue dispersion | $\phi''_{tissue} \approx n \cdot z \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_0^{3}}{2\pi c^{2}} \cdot \rho(\lambda_0)$ | $\rho$ = dispersion coefficient | Critical in 800 nm regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Triangle:** In UHR-OCT, three quantities are tightly coupled — **bandwidth (Δλ) ↑ ⇒ axial resolution (Δz) ↓**, but **sensitivity ↓** and **penetration ↓** (broader spectra are more strongly absorbed/scattered). The UHR engineer must balance this triangle.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

UHR-OCT is the imaging engine behind:

- **Ophthalmology** — visualizing **photoreceptor layer**, **external limiting membrane**, **drusen in age-related macular degeneration (AMD)**.
- **Dermatology** — non-invasive histology-equivalent images of **epidermis/dermis junction**, melanoma margins.
- **Cardiology** — resolving **fibrous cap thickness** in atherosclerotic plaques (vulnerable plaque detection).
- **Embryology / Developmental Biology** — in-ovo imaging of **Xenopus**, **zebrafish**, **Drosophila** embryos at cellular resolution.
- **Oncology** — intraoperative guidance for tumor-margin assessment.
- **Neuroscience** — cortical layer architecture in rodent models.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Axial Resolution from a Gaussian Spectrum

**Starting Point:** A broadband light source has a **Gaussian-shaped power spectral density** centered at $\lambda_0$ with FWHM $\Delta\lambda$:

$$S(\lambda) = S_0 \exp\left[-4 \ln 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda - \lambda_0}{\Delta\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Convert to frequency domain.**
Using $\nu = c/\lambda$, the Jacobian gives a Gaussian in frequency. To first order:

$$S(\nu) = S_0' \exp\left[-4 \ln 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\nu - \nu_0}{\Delta \nu}\right)^{2}\right]$$

where the FWHM in frequency is $\Delta\nu \approx (c/\lambda_0^{2}) \Delta\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Compute the complex degree of coherence** (the Fourier transform of the spectrum):

$$\gamma(\Delta z) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \, e^{-j 2\pi \nu \tau} \, d\nu, \quad \tau = \frac{2 \Delta z}{c}$$

Substituting the Gaussian $S(\nu)$:

$$\gamma(\Delta z) \propto \exp\left[-\pi^{2} (\Delta \nu)^{2} \tau^{2} / (4 \ln 2)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the envelope magnitude:**

$$\vert \gamma(\Delta z) \vert = \exp\left[-\frac{\pi^{2} (\Delta \nu)^{2}}{4 \ln 2} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \Delta z}{c}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Find the FWHM of this envelope.**
Set $\vert \gamma(\Delta z)\vert^{2} = 1/2$:

$$-\frac{\pi^{2} (\Delta \nu)^{2}}{4 \ln 2} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \Delta z_{1/2}}{c}\right)^{2} = -\ln 2$$

Solve for $\Delta z_{1/2}$:

$$\Delta z_{1/2} = \frac{c}{\pi \Delta \nu} \sqrt{\ln 2 \cdot \ln 2 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{1}{1}}$$

After careful algebra:

$$\boxed{\; L_c = \Delta z_{FWHM} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{c}{\Delta \nu} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda} \approx 0.44 \, \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda} \;}$$

**Step 5 — In-tissue correction:**
Tissue has refractive index $n \approx 1.36$ (typical for soft tissue at NIR). The optical path length is $n \cdot z$, so:

$$\Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{L_c}{n} = \frac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^{2}}{n \, \Delta \lambda}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Ti:Sapphire UHR-OCT at 800 nm

A typical Kerr-lens mode-locked **Ti:Sapphire laser** emits at central wavelength $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm with FWHM bandwidth $\Delta\lambda = 150$ nm.

**Step A — Compute in-vacuum coherence length:**

$$L_c = 0.44 \times \frac{(800 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{150 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Convert units to meters:

$$L_c = 0.44 \times \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-13}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$L_c = 0.44 \times 4.267 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$

$$L_c \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 1.88 \, \mu m$$

**Step B — Compute in-tissue resolution** (n = 1.38):

$$\Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{1.88 \, \mu m}{1.38} \approx 1.36 \, \mu m$$

This is **sub-cellular** — sufficient to image a retinal cone outer segment (~2 μm diameter).

**Step C — Compare to standard SLD-OCT** at $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm, $\Delta\lambda = 20$ nm:

$$L_c^{SLD} = 0.44 \times \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-13}}{2.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 14.1 \, \mu m \implies \Delta z_{tissue} \approx 10.2 \, \mu m$$

**Improvement factor: ~7.5× finer axial resolution.**

### 3.3 Dispersion Penalty Derivation — Why UHR is Hard

In broadband interferometry, **group velocity dispersion (GVD)** in optical components and in tissue causes different frequency components to arrive at different times, **broadening** the coherence envelope beyond the Fourier-transform limit.

The phase accumulated by an optical component of length $L$ with refractive index $n(\omega)$:

$$\phi(\omega) = n(\omega) \cdot \frac{\omega L}{c}$$

The Taylor expansion around $\omega_0$:

$$\phi(\omega) = \phi_0 + \phi' \cdot (\omega - \omega_0) + \frac{1}{2} \phi'' (\omega - \omega_0)^{2} + \frac{1}{6} \phi''' (\omega - \omega_0)^{3} + \cdots$$

- $\phi'$ = group delay (shifts envelope position)
- $\phi''$ = **Group Delay Dispersion (GDD)** [s²] — broadens the envelope
- $\phi'''$ = third-order dispersion (asymmetry)

**GDD-induced envelope broadening** (assuming Gaussian input):

$$\Delta z_{GDD} = \sqrt{(\Delta z_{source})^{2} + \left(\Delta z_{disp}\right)^{2}}$$

where the dispersion contribution is:

$$\Delta z_{disp} = \frac{\Delta \phi'' \cdot (\Delta \omega)^{2}}{2}, \quad \Delta \omega = 2\pi \Delta \nu$$

For tissue at 800 nm: $\phi''_{tissue} \approx 0.05 \, \text{ps}^{2}/\text{mm}$, and for a depth of $z = 1$ mm:

$$\phi''_{total} = 0.05 \, \text{ps}^{2}$$

With $\Delta \lambda = 150$ nm at $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm, $\Delta\nu \approx 7.0 \times 10^{13}$ Hz, so $\Delta \omega \approx 4.4 \times 10^{14}$ rad/s:

$$\Delta z_{disp} = \frac{0.05 \times 10^{-24} \cdot (4.4 \times 10^{14})^{2}}{2} \approx 4.8 \, \mu m$$

This is **3.5× larger** than the Fourier-limited resolution! Therefore:

> [!WARNING]
> **Dispersion compensation is NOT optional in UHR-OCT** — at sub-3 μm resolution, even 100 μm of unmatched glass path can destroy the resolution. Reference arm must contain a **matched dispersion element** (glass prisms, grating pairs, or fiber-Bragg gratings).

### 3.4 Sensitivity Derivation — The UHR Cost

The SNR in shot-noise-limited OCT detection is:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\eta P_{sample}}{h \nu \, \text{NEB}}$$

In UHR-OCT, the **broadband source power spectral density** $P_0/\Delta\nu$ is fundamentally lower than a narrowband source of the same total power, because $P_{sample} \propto R_s \cdot P_0$ and the noise floor depends on detection bandwidth.

For Ti:Sapphire (peak power ~10 kW pulsed), the **average power** is only ~10 mW. With 90/10 splitting and sample reflectivity $R_s = 10^{-4}$:

$$P_{sample} = 10 \, \text{mW} \times 0.10 \times 10^{-4} = 10^{-7} \, \text{W}$$

This gives SNR ~ 90 dB — adequate for retinal UHR, but **marginal for deeper tissues**, motivating the shift to **Spectral-Domain (SD) UHR-OCT**, which gains 20–30 dB in SNR through parallel detection.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Source Comparison for UHR-OCT

| **Source** | $\lambda_0$ [nm] | $\Delta\lambda$ [nm] | $\Delta z_{tissue}$ [μm] | Power | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SLD (Superluminescent Diode) | 800 | 20–60 | 6–18 | 1–10 mW | Cheap, fiber-coupled | Limited bandwidth |
| SLD (broadband) | 1060 | 50–110 | 3–8 | 5–20 mW | Good for retina | Modest resolution |
| Femtosecond Ti:Sapphire | 800 | 100–250 | **1–2.5** | 5–50 mW | Reference UHR source | Bulky, expensive, dispersion-sensitive |
| Cr:Forsterite | 1280 | 150–200 | **3–5** (1300 nm) | 10–30 mW | Deeper penetration | Lower resolution than Ti:Sapphire |
| Supercontinuum (photonic crystal fiber) | 800–1700 | 200–500 | **<1** (filtered) | 100 mW – 5 W | Tunable bandwidth | Noisy, expensive |
| Quantum-Dot / LED-like SLD | 1050 | 100+ | ~3 | 1–5 mW | Compact, stable | Coherence not perfect Gaussian |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UHR-OCT System Block Diagram (Time-Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Broadband Source\nTi:Sapphire 800 nm\nΔλ = 150 nm] --> ISO1[Optical Isolator\nprevents back-reflection]
    ISO1 --> FC1[2x2 Fiber Coupler\n50/50 splitting ratio]
    FC1 --> RA[Reference Arm\nScanning Mirror\nDispersion Compensator]
    FC1 --> SA[Sample Arm\nGalvanometer Scanner\nObjective Lens NA = 0.05]
    RA --> FC1
    SA --> FC1
    FC1 --> DET[Photodetector\nSilicon PIN or Avalanche]
    DET --> DAQ[Data Acquisition\nLock-in Amplifier]
    DAQ --> PROC[Signal Processor\nComputes A z envelope]
    PROC --> DISP[Display Cross-Sectional\nImage 1 μm axial]

    nodeA[Key Design Point]:::annot
    nodeB[Why UHR]:::annot
    nodeC[Resolution Driver]:::annot
    nodeD[Main Limitation]:::annot

    S1 -.- nodeA
    FC1 -.- nodeB
    RA -.- nodeC
    DET -.- nodeD

    classDef annot fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#888,stroke-dasharray:3 3
```

### 4.2 Spectral-Domain UHR-OCT (Modern Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Broadband UHR Source] --> FC[50/50 Coupler]
    FC --> REF[Reference Arm\nFixed Mirror\nMatched Dispersion]
    FC --> SMPL[Sample Arm\n2D Galvanometer\nHigh-NA Objective]
    SMPL --> TISSUE[Biological Tissue\nMultiple Layers]
    TISSUE --> SMPL
    REF --> FC
    FC --> SPEC[Spectrometer\nGrating 1200 lpmm\nLine-CCD 2048 pixels]
    SPEC --> FFT[FFT Processor\nk-space to depth]
    FFT --> IMG[En-face and B-scan\nDisplay]

    BW1[Spectral domain]:::callout
    BW2[Single-shot depth]:::callout
    BW3[Sensitivity gain]:::callout

    SPEC -.- BW1
    FFT -.- BW2
    SPEC -.- BW3

    classDef callout fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

### 4.3 Coherence-Gating Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Light from UHR Source] --> A2{Split into 2 arms}
    A2 --> A3[Reference Mirror\nknown depth zR]
    A2 --> A4[Sample Tissue\nunknown depth zs]
    A3 --> A5[Recombine at coupler]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6{Path difference Δz\nless than Lc?}
    A6 -- YES: Δz less than ~1.9 μm --> A7[Constructive Interference\nSignal Generated]
    A6 -- NO: Δz greater than Lc --> A8[No Interference\nNo Signal]
    A7 --> A9[Record depth-resolved\nreflectivity point]
    A8 --> A10[Move scanning mirror\nchange zR by Δz]
    A10 --> A6

    N1[Coherence Gate Width]:::info
    N2[In Tissue: 1.4 μm]:::info
    N3[Δz source Δλ]:::info

    A7 -.- N1
    A7 -.- N2
    A1 -.- N3

    classDef info fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#cc3333
```

### 4.4 Light Source Hierarchy for UHR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[UHR Light Sources] --> SLD[Superluminescent Diodes\nΔλ = 20-110 nm\nΔz = 3-15 μm]
    ROOT --> FS[Femtosecond Lasers\nTi:Sapphire, CrForsterite\nΔλ = 100-300 nm\nΔz = 1-3 μm]
    ROOT --> SC[Supercontinuum Sources\nPCF pumped by fs laser\nΔλ = 200-1000 nm\nΔz less than 1 μm]

    FS --> TISA[Ti:Sapphire 800 nm\nPhotoreceptor imaging]
    FS --> CRF[CrForsterite 1280 nm\nDeeper tissue]
    SC --> TUN[Tunable filter selects\nbandwidth region]

    SLD -.-> APP1[Standard OCT]
    FS -.-> APP2[UHR Research OCT]
    SC -.-> APP3[Multimodal UHR]

    H1[Resolution Hierarchy]:::hier
    ROOT -.- H1

    classDef hier fill:#f0fff0,stroke:#339933
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define Ultrahigh-Resolution Optical Coherence Tomography. What axial resolution values characterize UHR-OCT, and how do they differ from conventional OCT?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** UHR-OCT is a coherence-domain optical imaging technique that achieves sub-cellular axial resolution (typically 1–3 μm in tissue) by employing ultra-broadband, low-coherence light sources with spectral bandwidths of 100–500 nm FWHM.
>
> **Resolution range (1 mark):** UHR-OCT achieves 1–3 μm in-tissue axial resolution, while conventional OCT provides 10–15 μm resolution.
>
> **Distinguishing feature (1 mark):** The order-of-magnitude improvement arises solely from the **broader spectral bandwidth** of the source, which shortens the coherence length $L_c = 0.44 \lambda_0^{2}/\Delta\lambda$, while lateral resolution is governed independently by the objective NA.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*State and explain the formula for axial resolution in OCT. Why does UHR-OCT require broadband sources?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Formula (1.5 marks):** Axial resolution equals the coherence length of the source, given by
> $$\Delta z = \frac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^{2}}{n \, \Delta \lambda}$$
> where $\lambda_0$ is the central wavelength, $\Delta\lambda$ is the FWHM spectral bandwidth, and $n$ is the tissue refractive index.
>
> **Explanation (1 mark):** The axial resolution is set by the temporal coherence envelope (the Fourier transform of the spectrum). Broader bandwidth ⇒ shorter coherence time ⇒ finer axial resolution.
>
> **Why broadband (0.5 mark):** To achieve sub-3 μm resolution at near-infrared wavelengths, $\Delta\lambda$ must exceed ~100 nm, requiring femtosecond lasers or supercontinuum sources.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Below are two alternative questions, either of which may appear.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]

**(a)** Derive the expression for axial resolution in Optical Coherence Tomography starting from a Gaussian source spectrum. Show that the axial resolution is inversely proportional to the spectral bandwidth. (7 marks)

**(b)** A UHR-OCT system uses a Ti:Sapphire laser with central wavelength $\lambda_0 = 800$ nm and FWHM bandwidth $\Delta\lambda = 150$ nm. Calculate the **theoretical axial resolution** in (i) air, and (ii) tissue of refractive index $n = 1.38$. Compare this with a standard SLD-based OCT system with $\Delta\lambda = 25$ nm at the same central wavelength. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Gaussian source power spectral density:
$$S(\lambda) = S_0 \exp\left[-4 \ln 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda - \lambda_0}{\Delta\lambda}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Convert to frequency domain using $S(\nu)d\nu = S(\lambda)d\lambda$ and $\nu = c/\lambda$:
$$S(\nu) = S_0' \exp\left[-4 \ln 2 \cdot \left(\frac{\nu - \nu_0}{\Delta \nu}\right)^{2}\right], \quad \Delta\nu = \frac{c \, \Delta\lambda}{\lambda_0^{2}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute the complex degree of coherence as the inverse Fourier transform of $S(\nu)$:
$$\gamma(\Delta z) = \int S(\nu) \, e^{-j 2\pi \nu \tau} d\nu, \quad \tau = \frac{2 \Delta z}{c}$$
This yields a Gaussian envelope in $\Delta z$:
$$\vert \gamma(\Delta z) \vert = \exp\left[-\frac{\pi^{2} (\Delta \nu)^{2}}{4 \ln 2} \cdot \left(\frac{2 \Delta z}{c}\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Set $\vert \gamma \vert^{2} = 1/2$ to find the FWHM:
$$\Delta z_{FWHM} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{c}{\Delta \nu} = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^{2}}{\Delta \lambda}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Final boxed result with in-tissue correction:
$$\boxed{\; \Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^{2}}{n \, \Delta \lambda} \;}$$
**Conclude:** $\Delta z \propto 1/\Delta\lambda$ — broader bandwidth yields finer axial resolution. ∎

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Ti:Sapphire UHR-OCT resolution in air:**
$$L_c = 0.44 \times \frac{(800 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{150 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.44 \times 4.267 \times 10^{-6} = 1.88 \, \mu m$$
So $\Delta z_{air} \approx 1.88 \, \mu m$. ✓ (1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Ti:Sapphire UHR-OCT in tissue:**
$$\Delta z_{tissue} = \frac{1.88 \, \mu m}{1.38} \approx 1.36 \, \mu m$$
(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

**Step 3 (2 marks) — SLD standard OCT comparison:**
$$L_c^{SLD} = 0.44 \times \frac{(800 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}{25 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.44 \times 25.6 \times 10^{-6} = 11.26 \, \mu m$$
$$\Delta z_{tissue}^{SLD} = \frac{11.26}{1.38} \approx 8.16 \, \mu m$$
(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer)

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
UHR-OCT provides **~6× better axial resolution** than standard SLD-OCT for the same central wavelength, confirming the utility of broadband femtosecond sources.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a Time-Domain Ultrahigh-Resolution Optical Coherence Tomography (TD-UHR-OCT) system. Identify the key components and their roles. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the major challenges faced in UHR-OCT implementation, with special emphasis on (i) dispersion mismatch and (ii) sensitivity loss. Derive the dispersion-induced resolution penalty qualitatively. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Principle (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Block diagram (text-form):

```
Broadband Source (Ti:Sapphire, 800 nm, Δλ=150 nm)
        ↓
  [Optical Isolator]
        ↓
  [2×2 Fiber Coupler 50:50]
     ↙           ↘
[Reference Arm]  [Sample Arm]
  Scanning      Galvo Scanner +
  Mirror +      Objective Lens
  Dispersion    (NA = 0.05)
  Compensator
     ↘           ↙
  [Photodetector PIN/APD]
        ↓
  [DAQ + Lock-in Amplifier]
        ↓
  [Signal Processing → A(z) depth profile]
        ↓
  [Display: 2-D B-scan image]
```

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Light from the broadband source is split into reference and sample arms. Light backscattered from depth $z_s$ in tissue combines with reference light at the coupler. Interference occurs only when $|2z_s - 2z_R| < L_c$, producing a fringe pattern that is detected.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The reference mirror is rapidly scanned through depth; the demodulated envelope $|\gamma(2z_s - 2z_R)|$ yields the **reflectivity depth profile A(z)**. Multiple A-scans (across lateral scan) form a B-scan image.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Key components and roles:
- **Ti:Sapphire source:** provides ~1 μm coherence length
- **Dispersion compensator:** glass prisms/grating in reference arm to match tissue dispersion
- **Galvanometer scanner:** lateral beam steering for 2D imaging
- **High-NA objective:** determines lateral resolution (3–10 μm)
- **Silicon PIN or APD detector:** detects weak (nW-level) interference signals
- **Lock-in amplifier:** enhances SNR via heterodyne detection

**Part (b) — Challenges (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Dispersion Mismatch:**
Tissue introduces group delay dispersion (GDD) $\phi''_{tissue} \approx 0.05$ ps²/mm at 800 nm. The reference arm (typically air) has negligible GDD. This mismatch broadens the coherence envelope:

$$\Delta z_{measured} = \sqrt{(\Delta z_{source})^{2} + (\Delta z_{disp})^{2}}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Qualitative derivation:**
The accumulated spectral phase from dispersion is $\phi(\omega) = \frac{1}{2}\phi''(\omega - \omega_0)^2$. Different frequencies arrive with different group delays $\tau_g = d\phi/d\omega = \phi''(\omega - \omega_0)$. Over a spectral width $\Delta\omega$, the delay spread is $\delta\tau = \phi'' \cdot \Delta\omega$, contributing an extra axial spread:
$$\Delta z_{disp} \approx c \cdot \phi'' \cdot \Delta\omega / 2 = \frac{1}{2} \phi'' \cdot \Delta\omega^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta\omega} \cdot c \to \text{rearranges to:}$$
$$\Delta z_{disp} = \frac{1}{2} \Delta \phi'' \cdot (\Delta\omega)^2 / \omega_0 \quad \text{[order-of-magnitude expression]}$$

For typical UHR parameters, $\Delta z_{disp}$ can exceed $\Delta z_{source}$ by 2–3×, **destroying the UHR advantage** if uncompensated.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Sensitivity Loss:**
UHR sources have lower spectral power density $P_0/\Delta\nu$ for fixed total power. With $\Delta\lambda = 150$ nm, the power per unit bandwidth is ~7× lower than for a 20 nm SLD. SNR falls proportionally:
$$\text{SNR} = \frac{\eta R_s P_0}{h \nu \cdot \text{NEB}}$$
Combined with stronger tissue absorption at broader bandwidths, this limits UHR penetration to ~0.5–1 mm.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Matched dispersion** in reference arm (prism pairs, fiber Bragg gratings, or numerical post-processing via resampling)
- **Spectral-domain detection** (SD-OCT) for 20–30 dB SNR boost via parallel detection
- **Adaptive optics** to correct ocular aberrations
- **Wavelength selection** — choosing 1060 nm for deeper penetration over 800 nm peak resolution

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in UHR-OCT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the refractive index of tissue** — KTU examiners specifically check whether you divide the in-vacuum coherence length by $n \approx 1.36$–$1.4$. Omitting $n$ costs **2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Using wavelength instead of frequency bandwidth** — When converting $\Delta\lambda$ to $\Delta\nu$, students often write $\Delta\nu = c \Delta\lambda / \lambda$ instead of the correct $\Delta\nu = c \Delta\lambda / \lambda_0^{2}$. The squared $\lambda_0$ matters!
>
> 3. **Confusing axial vs. lateral resolution** — UHR-OCT only improves **axial** resolution. Lateral resolution is still diffraction-limited by NA. Stating otherwise loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring dispersion compensation** — A UHR-OCT answer that does not mention dispersion mismatch is **incomplete**. Examiners specifically look for it in 7-mark derivations.
>
> 5. **Mixing up coherence length and coherence time** — $L_c$ is in **meters**, $\tau_c$ is in **seconds**, with $L_c = c \tau_c$.
>
> 6. **Not specifying FWHM vs. RMS** — The Gaussian FWHM formula $L_c = 0.44 \lambda_0^2/\Delta\lambda$ is for **FWHM bandwidth**. Using a rectangular-spectrum formula in the wrong context loses marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — UHR-OCT**

- **Definition:** UHR-OCT = OCT with sub-3 μm axial resolution enabled by ultra-broadband sources.
- **Master Formula:** $\Delta z_{tissue} = \dfrac{0.44 \, \lambda_0^{2}}{n \, \Delta \lambda}$ — memorize and derive from scratch.
